            # Blueprint Reviewer Directive

iter183

## Scope

Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter completeness + correctness checklist.

4. **iter-181 HARD GATE history** — every chapter in the table above had

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212	
213	## Directive Format
214	
215	```markdown
216	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
217	
218	## Slug
219	<slug>
220	
221	## Strategy snapshot
222	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
230	
231	## Focus areas (optional)
232	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
233	
234	## Known issues
235	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
236	```
237	
238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
258	
259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
270	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
271	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
272	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
273	
274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
275	
276	```markdown
277	# Blueprint Review Report
278	
279	## Slug
280	<slug>
281	
282	## Iteration
283	<NNN>
284	
285	## Top-level summaries
286	
287	### Incomplete parts
288	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
289	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
290	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
291	
292	### Proofs lacking detail
293	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
294	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
295	
296	### Lean difficulty quality
297	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
298	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
299	
300	### Multi-route coverage
301	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
302	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
303	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
304	
305	### Citation discipline
306	<bullets naming chapter + label + which citation element is missing or suspect. Omit when zero findings.>
307	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: `% SOURCE:` line has no `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical — writer did not name the local file. Likely fabrication.
308	- `Bar.tex` / `\def:scheme`: `% SOURCE:` claims `(read from references/hartshorne-II-1.md)` but that file does not exist on disk. Fabrication; writer must dispatch a retriever or remove the citation.
309	
310	## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals
311	
312	<One proposal block per strategy phase with no or stub blueprint coverage. Each block is a complete chapter outline as specified in "Unstarted-phase proposals" above — not a one-line flag. Omit this section only when every phase has adequate coverage.>
313	
314	### Proposed chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`
315	
316	**Covers**: `<Lean file path(s)>`
317	**Strategy phase**: <phase name>
318	**Why now**: <one sentence>
319	
320	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
321	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <...>
322	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
323	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
324	
325	**`\uses` skeleton**:
326	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
327	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
328	
329	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
330	
331	**References for writer**:
332	- `references/<slug>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
333	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name>">
334	
335	**Subphase choices exposed**:
336	- <choice description and trade-off, or omit field if single decomposition>
337	
338	## Per-chapter
339	
340	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
341	- **complete**: true | partial | false
342	- **correct**: true | partial | false
343	- **notes**:
344	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
345	  - ...
346	
347	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex — complete + correct, no notes.
348	
349	(Cover every chapter. Chapters with findings get the full multi-line
350	block. Chapters that are `complete: true`, `correct: true`, with
351	no notes worth surfacing get the compact one-liner.)
352	
353	## Cross-chapter notes
354	
355	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
356	- ...
357	
358	(Omit if empty.)
359	
360	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
361	
362	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here.
363	
364	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>.
365	
366	## Severity summary
367	
368	Apply these rules verbatim:
369	
370	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
371	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
372	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
373	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter.
374	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
375	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels.
376	  - **Citation-discipline findings on blocks feeding an active prover route**.
377	  - **Unstarted-phase proposals** — each proposed chapter lands here as `unstarted-phase proposal: <phase name> — dispatch blueprint-writer for <proposed chapter filename> or record deferral`. These are must-act because an unwritten blueprint is an unparallelisable, unpreviewable phase; writing it early is the cheapest action in the loop.
378	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
379	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
380	  - Citation-discipline findings on blocks NOT feeding an active prover route.
381	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues.
382	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
383	
384	Overall verdict: one sentence. If unstarted-phase proposals exist, the verdict must name them — "N phases have no blueprint coverage; proposals provided for immediate writer dispatch" is a required component of the verdict when N > 0.
385	```
386	
387	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter. Unstarted-phase proposals do not block existing provers — they trigger blueprint-writer dispatch for future prover work. Do not under-classify them as "informational"; the project pays in deferred parallelism every iter a phase sits without a blueprint.
388	
389	## Return value
390	
391	Your final assistant message:
392	
393	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> chapters audited, <M> findings, <K> unstarted-phase proposals`
394	- Top-level summary counts (incomplete parts, proofs lacking detail, unstarted phases, etc.)
395	- The path to your full report.
396	
397	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
398	
399	## Reminders
400	
401	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
402	- **Cross-reference every strategy phase against existing chapters.** An unstarted phase is not a clean bill of health — it is an opportunity to write the blueprint now and enable parallelism.
403	- **Proposals are actionable, not informational.** Each proposal block must be concrete enough to serve as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Vague "chapter needed for X" notes are not proposals.
404	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
405	- **You audit against the directive's context**, not your own ideas of what the project should look like — but you ARE critical of weak prose and under-specified Lean hints.
406	- **You flag, you don't fix.** Even when the fix is obvious, the plan agent decides what changes next iter.
407	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the `k`-rational pointing of a smooth proper
9	geometrically irreducible curve `C / k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis.
10	End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms. **Char-general:** the
11	protected signatures take `[Field k]` only — NO `CharZero`. Any char-0
12	sub-case route is therefore PARTIAL only.
13	
14	The witness OBJECT `J` is always real (constructed unconditionally); only
15	`isAlbaneseFor` is universally quantified over `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`. Spine:
16	**pointed vs. unpointed**. `genus C := \dim_k H^1(C, O_C)` (arithmetic
17	genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).

66	Genus-0 arm = **Route C (Milne §I.3 rigidity)** — `J = Spec k` trivial,
67	char-general.

72	A.4 decomposed per table. A.4.a = dominant risk; A.4.b independently

2	
3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor (RR.2$_\ast$)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of $\mathscr L(D)$; Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf $\mathcal O_X(D)$ from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
29	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \Div(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor $D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]$ to its associated invertible
39	sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ on $C$. The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	$\chi$-identity proof of \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor $D$ via the
51	``$D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much $\chi$-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}) constructs
70	the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$ associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	$P \in C$ directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(D)$ at $D = [P]$ as canonically
74	isomorphic to \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via the
77	$\chi$-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of $C$ both reference $\mathcal O_C(D)$ for general $D$ (not
84	just $D = [P]$): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	$D \in \Div(C)$ and reads off $\deg(D)$ from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	\Cref{def:sheafOf}).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
92	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group $\Div(C)$ is the one defined in
103	\Cref{def:codim1_cycles} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is \Cref{def:prime_divisor}; the order
105	$\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor $Q$ is \Cref{def:order_at_point}; the
106	principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a non-zero rational function is
107	\Cref{def:principal_divisor}; the closed-point divisor $[P]$ is
108	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, and the present chapter's
111	\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} makes the comparison
112	$\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$:
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData $\to$ SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf $D$} as well, and \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} verifies
127	that the general construction at $D = [P]$ recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	$\mathscr L(D)$) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general $D$ and at the $D = [P]$
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	$\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
135	
136	\section{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor, def:order_at_point,
143	        def:principal_divisor}
144	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
145	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
146	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
147	  % (Cartier $\leftrightarrow$ Weil correspondence on an integral
148	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of $\mathscr L(D)$ as a
149	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field $K$). Read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
151	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
152	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
153	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
154	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
155	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
156	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
157	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
158	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, so $f_i^{-1}$ and
159	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
160	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
161	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If $X$ is an integral
162	  % scheme, the homomorphism $\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X$ of
163	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
164	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of $\mathscr K$, which
165	  % in this case is the constant sheaf $K$, where $K$ is the function
166	  % field of $X$."
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
168	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on $X$ and
169	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes $Y$, i.e., subschemes
170	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
171	  % Note also that if $X$ is an integral separated noetherian locally
172	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
173	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
174	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
175	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
176	  $\mathscr L(D)$), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-$K$
177	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
178	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
179	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
180	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
181	  $\bar k$ and let $D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor
182	  on $C$, with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors $Q$ of $C$ and
183	  supported on a finite set $\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}$
184	  (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The \emph{invertible sheaf
185	  $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to $D$}, also written
186	  $\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)$ in Hartshorne's notation, is the
187	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the constant sheaf
188	  $\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)$ (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
189	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
190	  field) defined section-wise by
191	  \[
192	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
193	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
194	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
195	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
196	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
197	  \]
198	  for each open $U \subseteq C$. Restriction along $V \hookrightarrow U$
199	  is the identity on the underlying carrier $K(C)$, which preserves
200	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
201	  the constraint set on $U$ embeds into the constraint set on $V$). The
202	  $\bar k$-module structure on
203	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is restricted from the
204	  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$ (a $\bar k$-scalar multiple of a
205	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
206	
207	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
208	  formal sum $D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]$ packages, on a curve where prime
209	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
210	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness $+$ dimension~$1$), the Cartier-divisor
211	  data $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
212	  open $U_i$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ in the support of
213	  $D$, pick a uniformiser $\pi_Q \in K(C)$ at $Q$ and set
214	  $f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}$; on opens disjoint from the support of $D$, set
215	  $f_i := 1$. The transitions on overlaps $U_i \cap U_j$ are units in
216	  $\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}$ for each prime $Q$ in the overlap (any
217	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
218	  $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
219	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original $D$. The
220	  ``$f_i^{-1}$-generated'' subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$ from Hartshorne's
221	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
222	  the local generator $f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}$ has order $-n_Q$ at $Q$,
223	  so the local sections are exactly $f \in K(C)$ with
224	  $\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$, on
225	  $U_i$.
226	
227	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
228	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP})
229	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
230	  \mathcal K_C$ is an invertible sheaf on $C$, independently of the
231	  choice of uniformisers $\pi_Q$ (any two choices differ by a unit, so
232	  they generate the same $\mathcal O_C$-submodule).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
235	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
236	  divisor $D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}$ and returns a
237	  $\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ on
238	  $C.\text{left.toTopCat}$, where
239	  $J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}$ is the
240	  standard topology on the topological space underlying $C$. The
241	  closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$ is
242	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of
243	  \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}; their agreement at $D = [P]$ is
244	  \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below. The construction follows
245	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
246	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
247	  $\bar k$-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
248	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
249	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
250	\end{definition}
251	
252	\section{Immediate corollaries}
253	
254	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles}
258	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  $\bar k$. Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  $0 \in \Div(C)$ is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
265	  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
270	  At $D = 0$ the coefficient $n_Q = 0$ at every prime divisor $Q$, so the
271	  section condition of \Cref{def:sheafOf} reduces, on every open
272	  $U \subseteq C$, to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme $C$ (the regular functions on $U$ are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  $C$ landing in $U$; this is the standard ``non-negative order $=$
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$. The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on $K(C)$, so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$.
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
290	\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}]
291	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
294	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles}
295	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  $[P] \in \Div(C)$ via \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ of \Cref{def:sheafOf} agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ of \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
306	  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
311	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point}
312	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
313	  $[P]$ has coefficients $n_Q = 1$ if $Q = P$ and $n_Q = 0$ otherwise
314	  (where $P$ is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
315	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Thus
316	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ contains $0$ and those
317	  $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ satisfying $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime
318	  divisor $Q \in U$ with $Q \neq P$, plus $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$ if
319	  $P \in U$.
320	
321	  On the \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} side, the same conditions
322	  are exactly the ones extracted by
323	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} of
324	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open $U$ (not only at the global section
325	  $U = C$): a rational function $f \in K(C)$ is a section of
326	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ on $U$ iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
327	  $Q \in U \setminus \{P\}$ and has at most a simple pole at $P$ when
328	  $P \in U$.
329	
330	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on $K(C)$. The two
331	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ are therefore equal
332	  section-by-section as subsets of $K(C)$, and the equality propagates
333	  to an equality of sheaves of $\mathcal O_C$-modules. The
334	  $\bar k$-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
335	  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$, so the identification is
336	  $\bar k$-linear on each section.
337	\end{proof}
338	
339	\begin{lemma}[Short exact sequence for $D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
343	        def:divisor_closed_point}
344	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
345	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any
346	  % point...''). Read from
347	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
348	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
349	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
350	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
351	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
352	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
353	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
354	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
355	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
356	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$.
357	  % (Since $\mathscr L(D + P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
358	  % it does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$.)"
359	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
360	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
361	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
362	  $\bar k$, let $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor, and let $P \in C$ be
363	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
364	  \Cref{def:prime_divisor} on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
365	  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ of
366	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ fits into a short exact
367	  sequence
368	  \[
369	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
372	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
373	  \]
374	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (equivalently, in
375	  $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$), where $k(P)$ denotes the
376	  skyscraper sheaf at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$ (the residue field of $P$
377	  is $\bar k$ since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $P$ is a
378	  $\bar k$-rational closed point).
379	\end{lemma}
380	
381	\begin{proof}
382	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
383	        lem:sheafOf_singlePoint, def:order_at_point}
384	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
385	  proceeds in two beats:
386	
387	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at $P$).} The closed-subscheme
388	  inclusion $P \hookrightarrow C$ has structure sheaf
389	  $\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)$ (a skyscraper at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$),
390	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
391	  $\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])$ (the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
392	  $\mathcal K_C$ generated by a local uniformiser $\pi_P$ at $P$ and by
393	  $1$ away from $P$, i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
394	  principal closed subscheme $P$). This produces the structural short
395	  exact sequence
396	  \[
397	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
399	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
400	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
401	  \]
402	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$.
403	
404	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$).} The line bundle
405	  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is locally free of rank~$1$
406	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), \Cref{def:sheafOf}), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
407	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is exact on $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
408	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
409	  \[
410	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
411	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
412	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
413	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
414	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
415	  \]
416	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
417	  \begin{itemize}
418	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
419	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
420	      $\Div(C)$-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
421	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
422	      ring-theoretic identity
423	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
424	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
425	      rational functions, where $n_Q^{D}$ is the coefficient of $Q$
426	      in $D$);
427	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
428	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
429	    \item $k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)$ (tensoring a
430	      skyscraper at $P$ by a locally free rank-$1$ sheaf preserves the
431	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over $P$ the line bundle
432	      $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P$ is a free $\mathcal O_{C,P}$-module of
433	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
434	      \cong k(P)$ is just $k(P)$; away from $P$ the skyscraper is the
435	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
436	  \end{itemize}
437	  Substituting yields
438	  \[
439	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
440	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
441	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
442	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
443	  \]
444	  exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, as required.
445	
446	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
447	  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is the
448	  inclusion of subsheaves of $\mathcal K_C$: at the level of sections,
449	  the constraint $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ of
450	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ implies the weaker constraint
451	  $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1$ of $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
452	  when $P \in U$ (constraints on $Q \neq P$ are unchanged), and the map
453	  is identity on $K(C)$ at the carrier level. The second map
454	  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)$ is the evaluation
455	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at $P$'' (i.e.\ the
456	  $\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}$-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of $f$
457	  at $P$), which is well-defined on $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
458	  modulo the subgroup of sections with $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ ---
459	  i.e.\ modulo $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
460	\end{proof}

462	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
463	
464	The construction of \Cref{def:sheafOf} is presheaf-by-hand: a
465	per-open $\bar k$-submodule of $K(C)$, with restriction along
466	$V \hookrightarrow U$ given by the identity on $K(C)$. This section
467	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
468	target category $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
469	
470	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For $V \subseteq U$, the
471	constraint set defining $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is a \emph{subset}
472	of the constraint set defining $\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$ (fewer
473	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on $K(C)$ restricts
474	to a well-defined inclusion
475	$\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
476	Composition $W \subseteq V \subseteq U$ is again identity-on-$K(C)$, so
477	strict functoriality holds.
478	
479	\paragraph{$\bar k$-linearity.} A $\bar k$-scalar $\lambda \in \bar k$
480	acts on $K(C)$ by multiplication, and
481	$\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)$ for $\lambda \neq 0$
482	(units have order zero); for $\lambda = 0$, $\lambda \cdot f = 0$ is
483	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
484	$\bar k$-submodule and the restriction maps are $\bar k$-linear.
485	
486	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
487	``$\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$'' is
488	\emph{stalk-local} at each $Q$: it depends only on the germ of $f$ at
489	the point of $C$ underlying $Q$, equivalently on the image of $f$ in
490	the DVR $\mathcal O_{C, Q}$. Consequently the sheaf property reduces
491	to two ingredients:
492	\begin{enumerate}
493	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is a
494	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at $K(C)$.} The constant
495	    presheaf at $K(C)$ is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
496	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
497	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
498	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
499	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
500	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
501	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
502	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
503	    prime divisor.
504	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
505	    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ to
506	    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})$.} The categorical forget
507	    $\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}$ creates limits, hence
508	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of $\bar k$-modules is a
509	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
510	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
511	    $\bar k$-module-valued $\mathcal O_C(D)$.
512	\end{enumerate}
513	
514	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open $U \subseteq C$ disjoint from
515	the support of $D$, the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
516	constraint $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$; hence
517	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U$, and the trivialisation
518	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U$ is the identity. On a small
519	open $U \subseteq C$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ from the
520	support of $D$, the local generator $\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)$ trivialises
521	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U$ as a free rank-$1$ $\mathcal O_C|_U$-module: a
522	section $f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
523	-n_Q$ iff $\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
524	``multiplication by $\pi_Q^{n_Q}$'' map is an
525	$\mathcal O_C|_U$-linear isomorphism
526	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U$. The two types
527	of trivialising opens cover $C$ (any prime divisor lies in at most
528	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of $D$ is
529	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
530	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
531	the cocycle is in $\mathcal O_C^{\times}$ and the sheaf is invertible.
532	
533	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
534	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
535	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
536	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
537	landed the analogous bottom-up
538	$\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$-valued construction for the
539	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
540	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
541	\texttt{RR.1} (\Cref{def:order_at_point} and surrounding lemmas).
542

569	    (\Cref{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}) — the inductive-step short

571	    \texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero} and
