582	\section{Out of scope}
583	
584	\begin{itemize}
585	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
586	    isomorphism $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when
587	    $D \sim D'$ (i.e.\ $D - D' = \div(f)$ for some
588	    $f \in K(C)^{\times}$) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
589	    It is not needed for the genus-$0$ critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
590	    consumes only $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))$ and the SES additivity at
591	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
592	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
593	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in $\Div(C)$ rather than
594	    in $\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \Div(C) / \sim$. The
595	    Cartier-divisor $\leftrightarrow$ \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
596	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
597	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
598	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
599	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
600	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
601	    \Cref{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add} at the single instance
602	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
603	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
604	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
605	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
606	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
607	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
608	  \item \textbf{Pullback of $\mathcal O_C(D)$ along a morphism.} The
609	    pullback $f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)$ for
610	    a morphism of curves $f : C' \to C$, used in degree-computation
611	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
612	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-$0$ critical path.
613	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
614	    space.} The identification
615	    $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)$ for a general Weil divisor
616	    $D$ (i.e.\ the analogue of
617	    \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} for general
618	    $D$) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
619	    case $D = [P]$ is the only specialisation consumed by the
620	    genus-$0$ critical path, and it is handled in
621	    \texttt{RR.3} via \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}.
622	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
623	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
624	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme
625	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ critical path
626	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
627	    does not engage them.
628	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
4

7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
32	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
33	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
34	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
35	derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
36	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
37	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
38	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that $\mathbb P^1$ is
39	\emph{already complete} and $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
40	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
41	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut} (the project's
42	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
43	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
44	\mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (the M\"obius scaling fixing both $0$ and
45	$\infty$), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
46	G_m$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising $f(0) = \eta_A$ and composing $h := \sigma_\times
47	\fatsemi f$, one applies additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
48	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
49	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ proper, $W = \mathbb G_m$, and
50	base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$. Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of
51	scaling, the $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)$ is
52	\emph{constant} at $\eta_A$, so the decomposition collapses to $h = p \fatsemi f$, i.e.\
53	$f(\lambda x) = f(x)$. Specialising $x = 1$ gives that $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant, and since
54	$\mathbb G_m$ is dense in $\mathbb P^1$ with $A$ separated, $f$ is constant
55	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
56	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
57	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
58	$\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$, and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
59	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive
60	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility
61	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
62	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-$0$ critical path. The corollary that a
63	pointed regular map of abelian
64	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
65	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-$0$ path; Milne's
66	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
67	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-$0$ critical path
68	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
69	gives that every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant
70	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-$0$
71	curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}, $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the
72	headline \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
73	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
74	neither Serre duality nor the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is
75	fully decoupled from Route~A.
76	
77	\medskip
78	
79	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
80	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
81	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
82	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
83	
84	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
85	
86	\begin{lemma}
87	
88	\leanok
89	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
90	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
92	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
93	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
94	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
95	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
96	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
97	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
98	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
99	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
100	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
101	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
102	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
103	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
104	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
105	\end{lemma}
106	
107	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
108	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
109	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
110	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
111	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
112	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
113	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
114	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
115	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
116	% assertion."
117	\begin{proof}
118	
119	\uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
120	\leanok
121	% NOTE (iter-159 review): the iter-157 unsoundness REMAINS REPAIRED. The collapse hypothesis
122	% (_hf : f(X x {y0}) = {z0}) is threaded through all three helpers (rigidity_lemma ->
123	% rigidity_core -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) and GENUINELY CONSUMED in the body of
124	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open (proves y0 \notin G, the load-bearing non-emptiness of V := Y - G).
125	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).
126	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open is sorry-free in its OWN body (hfib, the pullback-fibre fact over the
127	% k-bar-point y0, is closed). rigidity_snd_lift and snd_left_isClosedMap are axiom-clean (no sorryAx).
128	% NOTE (iter-160): the \uses edges run FORWARD along the true Lean dependency order
129	% (rigidity_lemma -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open -> rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine), so the
130	% not-proven status of the residual sorry propagates up and de-launders BOTH thm:rigidity_lemma and
131	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.
132	% NOTE (iter-161): bridge 2 (rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine) was DECOMPOSED into two named
133	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:
134	%   - Step 2, morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints: "two morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a
135	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
136	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
137	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
138	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual
139	%     (bridge 2's body is real assembly: Step 2 proven, the JacobsonSpace U instance now derivable).
140	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
141	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
142	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
143	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
144	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
145	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
146	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.
147	
148	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
149	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
150	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
151	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
152	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
153	
154	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
155	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
156	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
157	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
158	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
159	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
160	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
161	
162	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
163	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
164	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
165	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
166	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
167	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
168	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
169	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
170	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
171	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
172	\end{proof}
173	
174	\begin{remark}
175	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
176	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
177	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
178	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
179	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
180	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
181	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
182	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
183	  irreducible.)
184	\end{remark}
185	
186	\begin{remark}
187	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}
188	  The Lean formalisation of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} splits into three helper steps, mirroring
189	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
190	  collapsed map $(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$ followed by $f$ with $g \circ p_2$);
191	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
192	  reduced $X \times Y$ into the separated $Z$ that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
193	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
194	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
195	  open $X \times V$ and the slice-constancy of $f$ on it). The collapse hypothesis
196	  $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$ must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
197	  what makes $V$ non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
198	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).
199

201	  helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap}. The fibre-over-$y_0$ fact is \textbf{closed} (via the
202	  coarse \texttt{PullbackCarrier} layer), so \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open} is
203	  \texttt{sorry}-free in its own body. Bridge~2 (slice-constancy on the saturated open $U$,
204	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}) has now been \textbf{decomposed} along its
205	  cohomology-free route-(c) split into two named sub-lemmas: the globalisation
206	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced
207	  Jacobson source $\Rightarrow$ equal'') is \textbf{proven axiom-clean}, and the per-closed-slice
208	  constancy \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine} (Step~1, Mumford's slice
209	  argument) is the chain's \emph{single genuinely-deep residual} \texttt{sorry}. The body of
210	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} is therefore real assembly (Step~2 over Step~1).
211	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) STALE as of iter-162 — Step~1 is now PROVEN and the WHOLE

233	\begin{lemma}
234	
235	\leanok
236	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
237	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
238	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
239	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
240	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
241	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
242	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
243	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
244	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
245	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
246	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
247	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
248	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
249	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
250	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
251	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
252	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
253	  Then there is a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq X \times Y$ on which $f$ agrees, as a
254	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
255	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
256	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
257	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
258	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
259	
260	  \medskip
261	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
262	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
263	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
264	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
265	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
266	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
267	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
268	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
269	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
270	
271	  \medskip
272	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
273	  source.} The prose already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
274	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
275	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field $\kappa(y)$ to be $\bar k$ itself, so that a
276	  proper integral slice has global sections $\Gamma = \bar k$ and maps to a single $\bar k$-point of
277	  the affine $U_0$; this requires $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$. Second, the globalisation of the
278	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
279	  closed points of the saturated open $U$ to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ $U$ to be a Jacobson space.
280	  Over the algebraically closed $\bar k$ this holds precisely because $X \times Y$ is \emph{locally of
281	  finite type} over $\bar k$ (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
282	  property of $\Spec \bar k$, and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
283	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$. Accordingly the formalization carries
284	  \emph{both} $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and
285	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ on this lemma (in addition to
286	  $[\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]$), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
287	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
288	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
289	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
290	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
291	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
292	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ --- the base-field variable is literally named $\mathtt{kbar}$ ---
293	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over $\bar k$, so the
294	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
295	\end{lemma}

297	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
298	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
299	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
300	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
301	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
302	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
303	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
304	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
305	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
306	% assertion."
307	\begin{proof}
308	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
309	  \leanok
310	  Let $U_0$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U_0$, and set
311	  $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a
312	  \emph{closed} map, so $G$ (the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$) is closed in $Y$. The
313	  collapse hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0$ gives
314	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, hence $y_0 \notin G$; this is the only place
315	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for $V := Y - G$ is non-empty precisely
316	  because $y_0 \in V$. For each $y \in V$ the proper variety $X \times \{y\}$ is mapped by $f$
317	  into the affine $U_0$, hence to a single point, necessarily $f(x_0, y)$. So for all $x \in X$,
318	  $y \in V$, we have $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$, i.e.\ $f$ and
319	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on the non-empty open $U := X \times V$. (In the Lean
320	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
321	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
322	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
323	
324	  \medskip
325	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
326	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
327	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
328	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
329	
330	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``$X$ complete $\Rightarrow p_2$ closed'' is
331	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
332	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on $X$, base-changed across the canonical pullback square
333	  to make $p_2$ universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
334	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites $(\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}$ to
335	  $\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}$ exactly. This step is already
336	  proven.
337	
338	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of $V$) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
339	  $X \times \{y_0\}$ over the $\bar k$-rational point $y_0$ is exactly the image of the slice
340	  section $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
341	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
342	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
343	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
344	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1$ and
345	  $s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0$ come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
346	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
347	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
348	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
349	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]$.
350	
351	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
352	  ``Proper connected slice into affine $\Rightarrow$ single point, glued to a morphism equality on
353	  $U$'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
354	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ statement: the
355	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no $H^0$ base change), and route~(c)
356	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
357	  stack is: for each closed point $y \in V$ one has $\kappa(y) = \bar k$ (alg-closed, finite type);
358	  the slice $X_y$ is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
359	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over $\bar k$
360	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
361	  extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring
362	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so $f$ and
363	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
364	  locally-of-finite-type $U$ (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
365	  locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
366	  hypothesis $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$), so the per-slice
367	  agreement globalises to all of $U$ via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
368	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
369	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
370	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
371	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
372	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
373	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
374	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
375	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
376	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
377	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
378	\end{proof}

380	\begin{lemma}
381	
382	\leanok
383	[Bridge~2: slice-constancy on a saturated open mapping into an affine]
384	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
386	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
387	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
388	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
389	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite
390	  % type, so [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom] is now a stated hypothesis of this lemma (and across the
391	  % chain). With it, the `JacobsonSpace U` instance is a routine discharge (LocallyOfFiniteType.
392	  % jacobsonSpace + JacobsonSpace.of_isOpenEmbedding), no longer an as-typed gap. The only `sorry`
393	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis
394	  % set is recorded in the statement and proof prose below (both [IsAlgClosed k̄] AND
395	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim
396	  % is retired.
397	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
398	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
399	  % $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
400	  % any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
401	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
402	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
403	  ``for each $y \in V$, the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
404	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $X$, $Y$, $Z$ be objects of
405	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ with $X$ proper, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible, reduced,
406	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over $\bar k$ (the formalization carries
407	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$, which makes the saturated open $U$
408	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
409	  consumes), and $Z$ separated. Let $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a $\bar k$-point
410	  $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
411	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$ for the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
412	  endomorphism. Suppose:
413	  \begin{itemize}
414	    \item $U \subseteq X \times Y$ is a $p_2$-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
415	      for a set $V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y$ (so $U$ contains the whole fibre $X_y$ over each of
416	      its points);
417	    \item $U_0 \subseteq Z$ is an \emph{affine} open;
418	    \item $f(U) \subseteq U_0$, i.e.\ $f$ maps $U$ into the affine $U_0$.
419	  \end{itemize}
420	  Then $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on $U$ as scheme morphisms:
421	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$.
422	\end{lemma}
423	
424	\begin{proof}
425	
426	  \uses{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
427	  \leanok
428	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each $y \in V$, the complete
429	  variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single
430	  point of $U$''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
431	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
432	  $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
433	  $H^0$ base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
434	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
435	
436	  \medskip
437	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
438	  Fix a closed point $x$ of $U$, lying over a closed point $y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$.
439	  Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type over $\bar k$, the residue field
440	  is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Because $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ is saturated, $U$ contains the
441	  \emph{entire} fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$; hence $f$ maps the proper integral slice $X_y$ into the
442	  affine $U_0$. By $\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$ the global sections $\Gamma(X_y)$ form a
443	  field, module-finite over $\bar k$ by $\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$, and
444	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into
445	  the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so the
446	  restriction of $f$ to $X_y$ factors through a single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$ --- necessarily the
447	  point $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)$. Thus $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$
448	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of $U$. This per-slice statement is the
449	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
450	
451	  \medskip
452	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
453	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-scheme $U$
454	  ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$ / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
455	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$). To turn ``$f$ and
456	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on $U$,
457	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
458	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
459	  $\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U$, whose range is dense --- and feeds
460	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (the same
461	  family $\mathtt{rigidity\_core}$ uses). This yields the morphism equality
462	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$ on all of $U$. The
463	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
464	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
465	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$); that bespoke build is now done in
466	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
467	\end{proof}

469	\begin{lemma}\leanok
470	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
471	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
473	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
474	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
475	  Let $W$, $Z$ be schemes with $W$ \emph{reduced}, $W$ a \emph{Jacobson space}, and $Z$
476	  \emph{separated}. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z$ be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
477	  $x \in W$, the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
478	  $\Spec \kappa(x) \to W$ (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
479	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$), then $g_1 = g_2$.
480	\end{lemma}
481	
482	\begin{proof}
483	
484	\leanok
485	  This is the ``dense closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
486	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
487	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
488	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
489	  dominant probe.
490	
491	  Form the coproduct $P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)$ and the morphism
492	  $\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W$ defined by $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$ of the residue-field points
493	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$. Its topological range contains every closed point of $W$:
494	  the inclusion $\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P$ composes with $\mathrm{probe}$ to
495	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ (the defining property of $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$), and the
496	  range of $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ is the singleton $\{x\}$
497	  ($\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}$). Because $W$ is a Jacobson space, the closed points
498	  are dense ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$, i.e.\ $\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W$), so
499	  the range of $\mathrm{probe}$ is dense and $\mathrm{probe}$ is \emph{dominant}.
500	
501	  Componentwise, $g_1$ and $g_2$ agree after $\mathrm{probe}$: precomposing with each coproduct
502	  inclusion $\iota_x$ reduces (by $\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}$) to
503	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$,
504	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point $x$. By the universal property of the coproduct
505	  ($\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}$) this gives $\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2$.
506	  Since $\mathrm{probe}$ is dominant and $Z$ is separated, $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ cancels it
507	  on the left to yield $g_1 = g_2$.
508	\end{proof}

510	\begin{lemma}
511	
512	\leanok
513	[Global-sections constancy: two $\bar k$-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
514	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
516	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
517	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
518	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
519	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
520	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $W$ be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
521	  morphism $w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k$ that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
522	  type. Let $g \colon W \to V$ be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme $V$. Then any two
523	  $\bar k$-points of $W$ --- i.e.\ any two sections $a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W$ of $w$ (so
524	  $a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1$ and $b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1$) --- satisfy $a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g$.
525	
526	  \medskip
527	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
528	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ collapses the finite extension $\Gamma(W)/\bar k$; integrality of
529	  $W$ makes $\Gamma(W)$ a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
530	  $\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}$ is what upgrades $\Gamma(W)$ to a \emph{field} (without it
531	  $\mathbb A^1$, with $\Gamma = \bar k[t]$ and two distinct $\bar k$-points, is a counterexample);
532	  $\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}$ gives finiteness of $\Gamma(W)$ over $\bar k$; and
533	  $[\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]$ is what lets $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$ pin the morphism by its ring map
534	  on global sections.
535	\end{lemma}
536	
537	\begin{proof}
538	
539	\leanok
540	  Because $W$ is integral and $w$ is universally closed, the global sections
541	  $\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)$ form a field ($\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$), and the structure
542	  ring map $w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)$ (the map $\mathtt{wk.appTop}$ after the canonical
543	  identification $\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k$) is integral / module-finite
544	  ($\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$). Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and
545	  $\Gamma(W)$ is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
546	  ($\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}$); thus $\Gamma(W) = \bar k$ and
547	  $w^\sharp$ is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
548	  $a$, $b$ left-invert $w^\sharp$ on global sections (they are sections of $w$), so they induce the
549	  \emph{same} ring map $a^\sharp = b^\sharp$ on $\Gamma(W)$ (cancel the epimorphism $w^\sharp$). A
550	  morphism into the \emph{affine} $V$ is pinned by its global-sections ring map
551	  ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), whence $a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g$. This realises, without
552	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
553	  quoted from Mumford above.
554	\end{proof}
555	
556	\begin{lemma}
557	
558	\leanok
559	[Integrality descends to a retract of an integral scheme]
560	  \label{lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
562	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
563	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
564	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
565	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
566	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
567	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
568	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
569	  Let $X \times Y$ be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
570	  $\bar k$). Suppose $X$ is a \emph{retract} of $X \times Y$: there is a section
571	  $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ of the first projection $p_1$, i.e.\ $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X$.
572	  Then $X$ is integral.
573	\end{lemma}
574	
575	\begin{proof}
576	
577	\leanok
578	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
579	
580	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X$ makes the pullback on global
581	  sections $p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)$ split
582	  injective (it is left-inverted by $s^\sharp$). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
583	  $\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)$ embeds $\mathcal O_X(X)$ into a reduced ring, so it too is
584	  reduced ($\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}$ on the split injection); hence $X$ is reduced.
585	
586	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection $p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X$ is surjective because it
587	  admits the section $s$ (every point of $X$ is $p_1(s(\cdot))$). The continuous image of an
588	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as $X \times Y$ is irreducible and $p_1$ is a
589	  continuous surjection onto $X$, the space of $X$ is irreducible.
590	
591	  Reduced and irreducible together give that $X$ is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
592	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
593	  $X_y \cong X$ must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
594	\end{proof}
595	
596	\begin{lemma}\leanok
597	[Step~1: per-closed-slice constancy of a proper slice into an affine]
598	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
600	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
601	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
602	  % $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
603	  % any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
604	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
605	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
606	  ``for each $y \in V$, the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
607	  With the data of \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} --- $\bar k$ algebraically
608	  closed, $X$ proper over $\bar k$, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible, reduced, and locally of
609	  finite type, $Z$ separated, a $\bar k$-point $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, a morphism
610	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$, a $p_2$-saturated open $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ mapping into an
611	  affine open $U_0 \subseteq Z$ --- fix a \emph{closed} point $x$ of $U$. Then $f$ and the collapsed
612	  map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ (with $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
613	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$, i.e.\ $(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$) agree at $x$
614	  after the residue-field probe $\Spec \kappa(x) \to U$:
615	  \[ U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi f)
616	     = U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)). \]
617	\end{lemma}
618	
619	\begin{proof}

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
11	% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight $=$ Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      $=$ localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point $z$ of a scheme $X$ has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at $z$}, written
37	$\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$, is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$. The
39	\emph{coheight} of $z$ in $X$, written $\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)$, is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of $X$: the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in $X$, where $\prec$ is the
43	strict specialisation order $x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x$.
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at $z$ counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of $X$ containing $z$ --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-$1$ use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-$1$ hypothesis on $Y$ alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a $\Spec$ with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-$1$ specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.
88	
89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
101	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
102	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
103	  Let $X$ be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
104	  let $U \subseteq X$ be an open subset. For any $z \in U$,
105	  \[
106	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
107	  \]
108	  where on the right $U$ carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
109	  z \in U\rangle$ denotes $z$ viewed as a point of $U$.
110	\end{lemma}
111	
112	\begin{proof}
113	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers $n$, of the
114	  set of $n$ for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
115	  $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in the relevant
116	  specialisation preorder (with $\prec$ denoting strict specialisation). It
117	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
118	  strict-generisation chains starting at $z$ in $X$ and those starting at the
119	  corresponding point of $U$.
120	
121	  \emph{Chains in $U$ embed into $X$.} The inclusion $U \hookrightarrow X$ is
122	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if $y \rightsquigarrow x$ in $U$
123	  then $y \rightsquigarrow x$ in $X$, and distinct points of $U$ remain
124	  distinct in $X$. Hence every strict-generisation chain in $U$ starting at
125	  $z$ lifts to one in $X$ of the same length.
126	
127	  \emph{Chains in $X$ starting at $z$ all lie in $U$.} Conversely, suppose
128	  $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ is a strict-generisation chain
129	  in $X$ with $a_0 = z \in U$. For each $i \geq 0$, the relation $a_i \succeq
130	  z$ (in the specialisation preorder) is $z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
131	  $a_i$ is in the closure of $\{z\}$ \emph{viewed from above}; in our
132	  convention the strict order $a \prec b$ records $b \rightsquigarrow a$
133	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so $a_i \succeq z$ in $X$ means
134	  $a_i \rightsquigarrow z$ in $X$. By the Mathlib lemma
135	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
136	  $a \rightsquigarrow z$ with $z$ in an open set $U$ forces $a \in U$, since
137	  $U$ is open and contains $z$, hence contains every point whose closure
138	  contains $z$ --- equivalently every $a$ with $a \rightsquigarrow z$),
139	  every $a_i$ lies in $U$. Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
140	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
141	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle$ in $U$ of the same length.
142	
143	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
144	  chains starting at $z$ (resp.\ at $\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$) and
145	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
146	\end{proof}
147	
148	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
149	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
150	
151	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
152	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
153	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
154	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
155	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
156	
157	\begin{lemma}
158	  [Coheight on $\Spec R$ equals height in $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R$]
159	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
160	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
161	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring and let $p \in \Spec R$ (viewed as a point of
162	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
163	  \[
164	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
165	  \]
166	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
167	  prime-ideal chains $\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}$
168	  ending at the prime $\mathfrak{p}$ corresponding to $p$.
169	\end{lemma}
170	
171	\begin{proof}
172	  The specialisation preorder on $\Spec R$ is the dual of the natural
173	  inclusion preorder on $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$. Concretely, for
174	  $p, q \in \Spec R$ with underlying primes $\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}$,
175	  the relation $p \leq q$ in the specialisation preorder (which is
176	  $q \rightsquigarrow p$ in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
177	  containment $\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}$. This is the content of
178	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
179	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
180	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on $\Spec R$
181	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R$).
182	
183	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of $p$ in $\Spec R$
184	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at $p$,
185	  i.e.\ ascending chains $p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in the
186	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of $\mathfrak{p}$ in
187	  $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$ (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
188	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
189	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
190	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
191	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
192	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
193	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
194	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
195	  identification $\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}$
196	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
197	\end{proof}
198	
199	\section{The coheight $=$ Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
200	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
201	
202	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
203	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
204	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
205	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
206	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
207	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
208	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
209	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
210	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
211	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
212	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
213	
214	\begin{theorem}
215	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
216	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
218	  \uses{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding, lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
219	  Let $X$ be a scheme and let $z \in X$ be any point. Then
220	  \[
221	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
222	  \]
223	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at $z$ equals the coheight of $z$ in
224	  the underlying topological space of $X$ (with respect to the specialisation
225	  preorder).
226	\end{theorem}
227	
228	\begin{proof}
229	  \uses{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding, lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
230	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
231	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
232	
233	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of $z$.} By
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
235	  applied to the open set $X$ itself, there exists an affine open
236	  $U \subseteq X$ with $z \in U$. Write $\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U$
237	  for $z$ viewed as a point of the subscheme $U$.
238	
239	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to $z$ inside $U$.} Since
240	  $U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ via the affine equivalence
241	  $U \simeq \Spec R$ (with $R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$), the point
242	  $\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$ corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
243	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
244	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,$\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$.
245	
246	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of $\Gamma(U)$ at
247	  $\mathfrak{p}$.} The classical affine-stalk identification says
248	  $\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}$. In Mathlib this is the
249	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
250	  $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ as a localisation of $R$ at $\mathfrak{p}$ in the
251	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
252	
253	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
254	  $\mathfrak{p}$.} The Mathlib lemma
255	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
256	  \[
257	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
258	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
259	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
260	  \]
261	  where $\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})$ is the prime-ideal height in
262	  $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$. By
263	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
264	  $\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}$ used in
265	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
266	
267	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from $U$ to $X$.} By
268	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to $R$ and the
269	  affine identification $U \simeq \Spec R$,
270	  \[
271	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
272	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
273	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
274	  \]
275	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
276	  $U \subseteq X$ promotes the right-hand side back to
277	  $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)$. Concatenating the equalities from
278	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
279	\end{proof}
280	
281	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1$}{KrullDimLE 1}}
282	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
283	
284	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
285	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
286	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
287	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-$1$ case used in
288	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
289	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
290	
291	\begin{lemma}
292	  [Coheight $= 1$ forces stalk $\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1$]
293	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
295	  \uses{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
296	  Let $X$ be a scheme and let $z \in X$ be a point with
297	  $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$. Then the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$
298	  has Krull dimension at most $1$, equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
299	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
300	\end{lemma}
301	
302	\begin{proof}
303	  \uses{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
304	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
305	  $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$.
306	  The Mathlib predicate $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R$ unfolds to
307	  $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1$, so the inequality $1 \leq 1$
308	  discharges the goal.
309	\end{proof}
310	
311	\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
312	\label{sec:coheight_consumers}
313	
314	\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
315	\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
316	The private helper
317	\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
318	inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
319	\texttt{sorry} of the form
320	\[
321	  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
322	  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
323	\]
324	After this chapter's
325	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
326	\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
327	two independent goals:
328	\begin{itemize}
329	  \item The right conjunct $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1$
330	    is discharged by
331	    $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$ (the standing codim-$1$ hypothesis
332	    on $z$) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
333	    pair $\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1$ (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
334	    and $\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1$ (also from the bridge, since
335	    $1 \leq 1$) gives equality.
336	  \item The left conjunct $\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$
337	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
338	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
339	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
340	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A $\to$ IsRegularLocalRing $A_{\mathfrak{p}}$} at
341	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is $\sim 200$--$300$~LOC of
342	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
343	    chapter).
344	\end{itemize}
345	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
346	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
347	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
348	
349	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
350	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
351	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
352	argument
353	\[
354	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
355	\]
356	at every use site. Because $Y$ is a prime divisor of $X$ (so
357	$\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1$ by the definition of a prime
358	divisor as a codim-$1$ irreducible closed subset),
359	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
360	automatically from the codim-$1$ hypothesis on $Y$, so the explicit instance
361	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-$1$ infrastructure.} Any subsequent
364	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-$1$ point has
365	Krull dim $\leq 1$'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-$1$
366	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
367	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
368	The instance form
369	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
370	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.
371

374

380	  \item \texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding} ---
