85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  \uses{def:genus}
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $\chi(\mathcal F)$
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
99	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $\mathcal F$
106	  be a coherent sheaf on $C$. The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  $\mathcal F$ is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  $\dim C = 1$, Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i \geq 2$, so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of $\mathcal F$ on the proper $\bar
120	  k$-scheme $C$ guarantees that both $H^0$ and $H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional $\bar k$-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  \Cref{def:genus}), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf $\mathcal F$ on $C$ and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's $\Module \bar k$-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper $\HModule \bar k$ (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) applied to a
131	  $\Module \bar k$-flavoured carrier built from $\mathcal F$, with
132	  $\dim_{\bar k}$ extracted via $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$. The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ and from the $\Ext$-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The $\ell$-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[$\ell$-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:eulerChar_curve}
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $l(D)$ and of the
149	  % "dimension of $|D|$") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote $\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))$ by $l(D)$,
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of $|D|$} is $l(D) - 1$. The number
153	  % $l(D)$ is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $l(D)$).}
155	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let
156	  $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The
157	  \emph{$\ell$-invariant of $D$} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the $\bar k$-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to $D$. Finiteness of
164	  $\ell(D)$ is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper $\bar k$-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system $|D|$} has
167	  $\bar k$-dimension $\ell(D) - 1$ as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; $\ell(D)$ is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  $D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}$ (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}) and returns
174	  $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))$,
175	  computed through the same $\Module \bar k$-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve}. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference $\mathcal O_C(D)$ by name and consume only its
180	  $H^0$ functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
182	
183	\section{The $\chi$ identity: $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$}
184	
185	\begin{theorem}
186	
187	
188	\leanok
189	[Euler-characteristic identity for $\mathcal O_C(D)$]
190	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
192	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
193	        thm:divisor_degree_hom}
194	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
195	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any $D$, $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
196	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
199	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
200	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
202	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
203	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
204	  % is the Euler characteristic
205	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
206	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
207	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
208	  $\bar k$ of genus $g = g(C)$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$. Then
209	  \[
210	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
211	  \]
212	\end{theorem}
213	
214	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case $D = 0$. Then our
215	% formula says
216	% $\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g$,
217	% because $H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k$ for any projective variety
218	% (I, 3.4), and $\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g$ by (1.1).
219	% Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point. We will show
220	% that the formula is true for $D$ if and only if it is true for
221	% $D + P$. Since any divisor can be reached from $0$ in a finite number
222	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
223	% the result holds for all $D$.
224	% We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its structure sheaf is
225	% the skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$, which we denote by
226	% $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$ by (II, 6.18).
227	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
228	% $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
229	% Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
230	% $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
231	% (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
232	% does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$). Now the Euler characteristic is
233	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
234	% = 1$, so we have
235	% $\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1$.
236	% On the other hand, $\deg D + P = \deg D + 1$, so our formula is true
237	% for $D$ if and only if it is true for $D + P$, as required."
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
240	        thm:divisor_degree_hom, def:divisor_closed_point}
241	  The proof is by induction on the structure of $\Div(C)$ as the free
242	  abelian group on closed points (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles},
243	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Both sides of the asserted
244	  equation are additive in $D$ (the right-hand side by linearity of
245	  $\deg$, \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}; the left-hand side by the
246	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
247	  for $D = 0$ and to show that it transports across the elementary
248	  modification $D \mapsto D + [P]$ at any closed point $P \in C$.
249	
250	  \emph{Base case $D = 0$.} The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(0)$ is the
251	  structure sheaf $\mathcal O_C$, and the formula
252	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g$ reads
253	  \[
254	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
255	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
256	    \;=\; 1 - g.
257	  \]
258	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
259	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g$ (\Cref{def:genus}). The
260	  first term is $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$: $C$ is a
261	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
262	  closed field $\bar k$, so its connected components agree with its
263	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
264	  \cong \bar k$ as $\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
265	  statement that ``$H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k$ for any projective
266	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
267	  the project's $H^0$-bridge in
268	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
269	  two values gives $1 - g = 1 - g$, as required.
270	
271	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``$D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P$''
272	  step).} Let $P \in C$ be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
273	  argument: view $P$ as a closed subscheme of $C$; its structure
274	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$ supported at $P$,
275	  and its sheaf of ideals is $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C$
276	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out $P$ in $C$). Hence
277	  the short exact sequence
278	  \[
279	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
280	  \]
281	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
282	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (a locally free
283	  $\mathcal O_C$-module of rank $1$, so tensoring preserves exactness
284	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged) yields
285	  \[
286	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
287	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
288	    \to k(P) \to 0.
289	  \]
290	  Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence
291	  of coherent sheaves (Mathlib's preadditive-category Euler
292	  characteristic; see the Lean reference note at the end of this
293	  proof) gives
294	  \[
295	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
296	    \;=\;
297	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
298	  \]
299	  The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ has
300	  $H^0(C, k(P)) \cong \bar k$ and $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$ (the cohomology
301	  of a flasque sheaf supported at a closed point is concentrated in
302	  degree $0$, with $H^0$ equal to the stalk $\bar k$), so
303	  $\chi(k(P)) = 1$. Therefore
304	  \[
305	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
306	  \]
307	  On the other hand, $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
308	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), so by
309	  \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
310	  \[
311	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
312	  \]
313	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
314	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ holds for $D$ if and only
315	  if it holds for $D + [P]$. Since the closed-point divisors $[P]$
316	  generate $\Div(C)$ as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
317	  from $D = 0$ to every $D \in \Div(C)$ by induction on the (signed)
318	  number of generators in the expression of $D$.
319	
320	  \emph{Lean reference note on $\chi$-additivity.} Mathlib (snapshot
321	  \texttt{b80f227}) provides the Euler-characteristic API
322	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
323	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar}, \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar})
324	  but does \emph{not} ship the specific additivity statement
325	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} [expected,
326	  not in Mathlib under that name]. The genus-$0$ proof of
327	  \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} therefore threads additivity of
328	  $\chi$ on a short exact sequence either as a project-side lemma
329	  proved inside \texttt{RRFormula.lean} (from
330	  \texttt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank} combined with
331	  the long exact sequence of $H^*$) or as a candidate Mathlib upstream
332	  PR. The base case $\chi(k(P)) = 1$ is closed by the Mathlib
333	  skyscraper-sheaf API together with the project's $H^0$-bridge
334	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}).
335	\end{proof}
336	
337	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
338	
339	\begin{theorem}
340	
341	
342	\leanok
343	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
344	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
346	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
347	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
348	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
349	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-$0$ specialisation
350	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
351	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
352	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
353	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
354	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
355	  % $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
356	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$.
357	  % We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so
358	  % conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two
359	  % distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
360	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg K - D = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have
361	  % $l(K - D) = 0$, and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of
362	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim
363	  % Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
364	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
365	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
366	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$ with
367	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$. Then
368	  \[
369	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
370	  \]
371	\end{theorem}
372	
373	\begin{proof}
374	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
375	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
376	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
377	  Specialising
378	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} to the genus-$0$
379	  hypothesis $g = 0$ yields
380	  \[
381	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
382	  \]
383	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} and the definition
384	  $\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
385	  (\Cref{def:l_invariant}), this rewrites as
386	  \[
387	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
388	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
389	  \]
390	  Under the standing genus-$0$ hypothesis and the assumption
391	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$, the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
392	  \[
393	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
394	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
395	  \]
396	  Substituting yields $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$, as required.
397	
398	  \emph{The $H^1$-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
399	  irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, the vanishing
400	  $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$ is the Hartshorne
401	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: $\ell(D) \neq 0$ forces
402	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$; equivalently, for a curve where every
403	  degree-$0$-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
404	  sections after a single point-twist, $H^1$ of the twist must vanish
405	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
406	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this $H^1$
407	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
408	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
409	  bundle $\mathcal O_C(D)$ and its cohomology are constructed; once
410	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
411	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
412	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
413	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
414	  $H^1$-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
415	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
416	  follow-up signature simplification.
417	
418	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
419	  this theorem is the genus-$0$ classification
420	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
421	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$
422	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
423	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
424	  produces a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via
425	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
426	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$, which is then upgraded to an
427	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
428	\end{proof}
429	
430	\section{Out of scope}
431	
432	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
433	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
434	
435	\begin{itemize}
436	  \item The general-$g$ Riemann--Roch theorem
437	    $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
438	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor $K$, the
439	    canonical sheaf $\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C$, and
440	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
441	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
442	    the genus-$0$ critical path does not require them; the general
443	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
444	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$
445	    from a Weil divisor $D$, the linear-equivalence isomorphism
446	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when $D \sim D'$, and
447	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
448	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
449	    $t$ exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
450	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
451	    of \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} the dependency on
452	    $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is reduced to its $H^0$ functor and the short
453	    exact sequence of the closed point.
454	  \item The vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for
455	    $\deg(D) \geq 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve is consumed as a named
456	    premise by \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}; its closure is
457	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
458	    $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)$ is computed) and is recovered for
459	    a general smooth proper genus-$0$ curve via the isomorphism
460	    $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
461	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
462	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
463	    curve over $\bar k$ with $g = 0$ is isomorphic to
464	    $\mathbb P^1$'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
465	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
466	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
467	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
468	    \deg(D) + 1$ identity); the other input is the degree-$0$
469	    principal-divisor identity \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} from
470	    \texttt{RR.1}.
471	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping ($\chi$ as an alternating sum
472	    over all $i \geq 0$, the Grothendieck vanishing
473	    $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i > \dim C = 1$) is not separately
474	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
475	    $\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1$ which is the standing
476	    curve specialisation.
477	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The rational-curve isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ (RR.4)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1 Example~1.3.5
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
8	% + IV \S2, opening of "Hurwitz's Theorem" (degree of a finite morphism of

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
18	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
26	\begin{itemize}
27	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
28	    group $\Div(C)$, the degree map
29	    $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ (\Cref{def:divisor_degree}), and the
30	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
31	    (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
32	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
33	    formula in genus zero $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$
34	    (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
35	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
36	    the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$, its global-sections identification
37	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2$, and the corollary
38	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
39	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
40	    \bar k \cdot 1$ on a genus-$0$ curve with a marked $\bar k$-point.
41	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
42	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$
43	    and a $\bar k$-rational point is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.''
44	\end{itemize}
45	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
46	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
47	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
48	the interface block \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
49	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
50	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
51	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
52	\Cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
53	
54	\paragraph{Scope of the $\bar k$-base classification.} The chapter restricts
55	to the case where the base field $\bar k$ is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
56	k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so the ``$C$ has a $\bar
57	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
58	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ is non-empty,
59	hence has closed points, all of which are $\bar k$-rational). The general
60	``$k \to \bar k$ descent'' step ``genus-$0$ curve over a non-closed $k$ with
61	a $k$-rational point $\cong \mathbb P^1_k$'' is a separate sub-build hosted
62	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
63	consumer) and is out of scope here.
64	
65	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
66	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
67	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. The Lean signature
68	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
69	project's standing curve typeclass package:
70	\begin{quote}
71	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
72	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
73	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
74	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
75	\end{quote}
76	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target
77	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} in
78	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290--296}. The genus
79	$g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of \texttt{Genus.tex},
80	namely $g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ via the project's
81	$\Module \bar k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology
82	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target
83	$\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is the project's
84	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar~kbar} of
85	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean}.

87	\section{The morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from two global sections}
88	
89	\begin{lemma}
90	
91	\leanok
92	[Morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from a globally generating pair of sections]
93	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
95	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
96	  Let $X$ be a scheme over a field $\bar k$, let $\mathcal L$ be an invertible
97	  sheaf on $X$, and let $s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)$ be two global
98	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
99	  \[
100	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
101	  \]
102	  (Equivalently, $s_0$ and $s_1$ generate $\mathcal L$ at every point.) Then
103	  there exists a unique morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
104	  \[
105	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
106	  \]
107	  whose pullback of $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)$ is $\mathcal L$ and whose
108	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
109	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))$ are $s_0$ and $s_1$ respectively. On the
110	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
111	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ the morphism $\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}$ is the map ``$x
112	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)$''; on the chart $D_+(x_1)$ it is the map ``$x
113	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
114	  $D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}$ by the cocycle identity
115	  $(s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1$.
116	\end{lemma}
117	
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
120	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
121	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to $\mathbb P^n$''), specialised to
122	  $n = 1$. Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
123	  under the name
124	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
125	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
126	
127	  \begin{quote}
128	  \small
129	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{$\sigma$ A\} [CommRing A]
130	    [SetLike $\sigma$ A] [AddSubgroupClass $\sigma$ A] ($\mathcal A$ :
131	    \(\mathbb N\) → $\sigma$) [GradedRing $\mathcal A$] \{X : Scheme\}\\
132	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj $\top$)\\
133	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant $\mathcal A$).toIdeal = $\top$) :\\
134	    \phantom{}\quad(X $\longrightarrow$ Proj $\mathcal A$)}
135	  \end{quote}
136	
137	  Specialising to $\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ with the standard $\mathbb
138	  N$-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded $\bar k$-algebra
139	  homomorphism $f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ and a
140	  proof that the irrelevant ideal $(x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ maps to
141	  the unit ideal of $H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
142	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
143	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of $\mathbb
144	  P^n$ as a $\mathrm{Proj}$). When $\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
145	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
146	  cover $X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)$, where on each chart the sections become
147	  honest global functions ($s_1 / s_0$ on $D(s_0)$, $s_0 / s_1$ on $D(s_1)$,
148	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
149	  project-bespoke wrapper
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
151	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
152	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
153	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
154	
155	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
156	  a separated scheme: two morphisms $\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1$
157	  with $\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i$ for $i = 0, 1$ agree on the basic-open
158	  charts $D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)$ (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
159	  the same rational function), and these charts cover $\mathbb P^1$, so the
160	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
161	\end{proof}
162	
163	\section{Degree of a morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via its pole divisor}
164	
165	% NOTE (iter-182 plan-phase Pin 2 signature strengthening). The iter-177
166	% file-skeleton placeholder existential
167	%   "$\exists (d : \mathbb N) (D : C.\mathrm{WeilDivisor}),\
168	%      0 < d \wedge D.\mathrm{degree} = d$"
169	% was vacuous in $\varphi$ (no constraint tying $D$ to $\varphi$ or the
170	% point $\infty$). The iter-182 plan-phase refactor (refactor slug
171	% `pin2-sig-strengthen`) replaces it with the substantive existential
172	% pinning $D$ to $\varphi^*[\infty]$ and the degree to the function-field
173	% extension degree $[K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$. The new typed-sorry def
174	% `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` (in
175	% `RationalCurveIso.lean`, analogous to the iter-181 typed-sorry def
176	% `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField` of OCofP.lean) carries the
177	% Weil divisor $\varphi^*[\infty]$; its body is queued for iter-183+
178	% via the affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route.
179	\begin{lemma}
180	
181	\leanok
182	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via the preimage of $\infty$]
183	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
184	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
185	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point}
186	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
187	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
188	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
189	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
190	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
191	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
192	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
193	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ be a non-constant morphism
194	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$. Then
195	  $\varphi$ is finite, its function-field extension
196	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$ (the canonical
197	  $\varphi$-induced map, supplied as an
198	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
199	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
200	  divisor $D \in \Div(C)$ such that
201	  \[
202	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
203	    \quad\text{and}\quad
204	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
205	  \]
206	  Equivalently, the morphism $\varphi$ satisfies
207	  \[
208	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
209	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
210	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
211	  \]
212	  where $t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)$ is a local parameter at $\infty$
213	  (equivalently $t_\infty = 1/u$ for the standard affine coordinate $u$ on
214	  $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$); and for any closed point
215	  $Q \in \mathbb P^1$ the same identification gives
216	  $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$.
217	
218	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
219	  $\varphi^*[\infty]$ is exposed in Lean as
220	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor $\varphi$}, a
221	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
222	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
223	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
224	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
225	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point $P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$
226	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
227	  $\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)$ from the maximal ideal at $\infty$ in
228	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$). On the Lean side, the existential output of
229	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
230	  $D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi$ together with
231	  $\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)$,
232	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
233	  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
234	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
235	\end{lemma}
236	
237	\begin{proof}
238	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point,
239	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
240	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$ is
241	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of $\varphi$ is a closed
242	  irreducible subset of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ that is not a point (else
243	  $\varphi$ would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
244	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (the only proper irreducible closed subset of $\mathbb
245	  P^1_{\bar k}$ is a point); hence $\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
246	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
247	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
248	  fact that $\varphi$ has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
249	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
250	  contradicting irreducibility of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $C$ at
251	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
252	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$
253	  is finite.
254	
255	  The identification $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$ for
256	  any closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$ is the multiplicativity of the
257	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
258	  $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
259	  \Div(C')$, $\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
260	  to $D = [Q]$ (a divisor of degree $1$ since $\kappa(Q) = \bar k$) gives
261	  $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)$. Equivalently, the degree of
262	  $\varphi$ equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
263	  P^1$, counted with the ramification multiplicities $\ord_P(\varphi^*
264	  t_\infty)$ at the preimage points $P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
265	
266	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
267	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
268	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
269	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
270	  and the multiplicativity of $\deg$ under composition of $\div$ and
271	  $\deg$; the closely related ``$\div(f)$ has degree zero'' specialisation
272	  is \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
273	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
274	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
275	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
276	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
277	\end{proof}

279	\section{A degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
280	
281	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
282	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
283	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
284	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to $\varphi$) was strictly
285	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
286	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
287	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
288	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
289	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
290	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
291	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
292	% only the input shape changes.
293	\begin{lemma}
294	
295	\leanok
296	[Degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
297	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
299	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
300	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
301	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
302	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
303	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
304	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
305	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
306	  % equivalent:}
307	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
308	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
309	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over $k$, and $k$-homomorphisms.}"
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
311	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
312	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
313	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
314	  (p.~299).}
315	  Let $C, C'$ be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
316	  algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ be
317	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
318	  degree one,
319	  \[
320	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
321	  \]
322	  formally
323	  $\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1$ in the Lean signature.
324	  Then $\varphi$ is an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
325	
326	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
327	  lemma, the $K(C')$-algebra structure on $K(C)$ used to interpret the
328	  degree $[K(C) : K(C')]$ is the canonical $\varphi$-induced
329	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
330	  \[
331	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
332	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
333	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
334	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
335	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
336	  \]
337	  of the germ at the image of the generic point $\xi_C \in C$ with the
338	  stalk-map of $\varphi$ at $\xi_C$ and the fraction-field lift at
339	  $\xi_C$ (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
340	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
341	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
342	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
343	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	  The function-field hypothesis $[K(C) : K(C')] = 1$ states that the
348	  canonical $\varphi$-induced map $\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)$ is a
349	  finite extension of degree one; since $K(C')$ is a field and the
350	  extension has dimension one as a $K(C')$-vector space, $\varphi^\#$ is
351	  an isomorphism of function fields $K(C') \cong K(C)$. (Equivalently, by
352	  the IV.2-opening identification $\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]$, this is
353	  the statement $\deg(\varphi) = 1$; the morphism $\varphi$ is finite in
354	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
355	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
356	  \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
357	
358	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment $C \mapsto \bar k(C)$ is
359	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
360	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
361	  one over $\bar k$ (with $\bar k$-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
362	  the isomorphism $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$ produced by the previous
363	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
364	  C'$, and this isomorphism agrees with $\varphi$ on the generic point (by
365	  construction: it is the inverse-image of $\varphi^\#$ under the
366	  equivalence). The two morphisms $C \to C'$ that agree on the generic
367	  point of the integral scheme $C$ agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
368	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
369	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence $\varphi$ is itself
370	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
371	
372	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
373	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
374	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-$1$ morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
375	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
376	  Indeed, the direct image $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C$ is a coherent
377	  $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
378	  1$, and an inclusion $\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
379	  \mathcal O_C$ of coherent rank-$1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
380	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
381	  $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}$ has trivial stalk. The
382	  quotient is a torsion coherent $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module of generic rank
383	  $\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0$, hence is itself torsion-free (a
384	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
385	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
386	  \mathcal O_{C'}$ and $\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
387	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
388	
389	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
390	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
391	  non-constant morphism $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ together with a
392	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
393	  (the canonical $\varphi$-induced function-field map) and the
394	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
395	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
396	  produces an isomorphism $C \cong C'$ in the slice category
397	  $\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}$. The proof body threads the Mathlib
398	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
399	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
400	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
401	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
402	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
403	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
404	\end{proof}

406	\section{Headline: genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
407	
408	\begin{theorem}
409	
410	\leanok
411	[Genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$]
412	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
413	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
414	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
415	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
416	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
417	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
418	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
419	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S1 Example~1.3.5 (genus-zero classification;
420	  % "rational $\iff g = 0$" + "rational curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ for
421	  % complete nonsingular curves") (read from
422	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314, doc p.~297).
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
424	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this
425	  % chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve $X$ is
426	  % rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now using
427	  % (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We
428	  % already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
429	  % suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two distinct
430	  % points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor $D = P - Q$.
431	  % Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$,
432	  % and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by
433	  % (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies
434	  % $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
435	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
436	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field, and let $C$ be a smooth
437	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$.
438	  Then there exists an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes
439	  \[
440	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
441	  \]
442	  Concretely, the Lean signature
443	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
444	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
445	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
446	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
447	  \Cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} is its consumer-side restatement,
448	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
449	\end{theorem}
450	
451	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
452	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
453	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
454	% "We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots"). The present
455	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
456	% (linear-system construction $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
457	% ([P]))$, degree-$1$ identification, isomorphism).
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
460	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
461	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
462	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
463	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
464	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ the smooth proper
465	  geometrically irreducible curve $C$ has closed points, all of which are
466	  $\bar k$-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
467	  $\bar k$ is a finite extension of $\bar k$, hence is $\bar k$ itself by
468	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such $\bar k$-rational point $P \in C$;
469	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
470	
471	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$.}
472	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
473	  (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
474	  effective divisor $[P]$ of degree $1$, we obtain
475	  $\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2$. The constant section
476	  $1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ accounts for a
477	  one-dimensional $\bar k$-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
478	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
479	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
480	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
481	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
482	
483	  \emph{Step 2: morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via the linear system $(1, f)$.}
484	  The pair $(1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2$ generates the
485	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ at every point of $C$: at points
486	  $Q \in C \setminus \{P\}$ the section $1$ does not vanish in
487	  $\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}$, and at the marked point
488	  $P$ the section $f$ does not vanish in $\mathcal O_C([P])_P$ (because $f$
489	  has a pole of order exactly $1$ at $P$ in the rational-function
490	  picture: $f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k$ with $\div(f)_\infty = [P]$).
491	  Therefore by \Cref{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
492	  exists a unique morphism
493	  \[
494	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
495	  \]
496	  with $\varphi^* x_0 = 1$ and $\varphi^* x_1 = f$ (so the pullback of
497	  the standard ratio coordinate $u = x_1 / x_0$ on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
498	  \{\infty\}$ is $f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
499	  $t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f$ is the local parameter $1/f$ at $P$).
500	
501	  \emph{Step 3: $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ via the pole divisor of $f$.} The
502	  morphism $\varphi$ is non-constant (since $f$ is non-constant as a
503	  rational function on $C$, the pullback $\varphi^* u = f$ takes
504	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant $\varphi$). By
505	  \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to $\varphi$,
506	  \[
507	    \deg(\varphi)
508	    \;=\;
509	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
510	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
511	  \]
512	  We compute the pole divisor of $f$ as a rational function on $C$:
513	  since $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ with $f$ non-constant and
514	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ the line bundle of the effective divisor $[P]$, the
515	  rational-function $f$ has pole divisor $(f)_\infty = [P]$ (the
516	  unique pole of order $1$ at $P$, no other poles; this is the standard
517	  identification of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$ with the rational functions
518	  $g \in K(C)$ satisfying $\div(g) + D \geq 0$, specialised to
519	  $D = [P]$). Hence $\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}$ with multiplicity~$1$
520	  and
521	  \[
522	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
523	  \]
524	
525	  \emph{Step 4: $\varphi$ is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
526	  identification $\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$, the
527	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
528	  $\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1$ (with
529	  the canonical $\varphi$-induced
530	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
531	   C.functionField]} instance). By
532	  \Cref{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
533	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (smooth proper
534	  geometrically irreducible curves over $\bar k$ on both sides), under
535	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, $\varphi$ is an
536	  isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
537	
538	  This produces the required isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$
539	  closing the headline target
540	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
541	
542	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
543	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
544	  $P, Q \in C$ and the degree-zero divisor $D = P - Q$, deriving $P \sim Q$
545	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that $C$ is rational; combined with I.6.12
546	  this yields $C \cong \mathbb P^1$. The present proof restates the same
547	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
548	  construction: instead of going through the $P \sim Q$ statement and
549	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
550	  $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1$ from the two sections $(1, f)$ of
551	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
552	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
553	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero $\Rightarrow$
554	  $\ell([P]) = 2$ $\Rightarrow$ non-constant $f$ $\Rightarrow$ degree-$1$
555	  map to $\mathbb P^1$ $\Rightarrow$ iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
556	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
557	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
558	\end{proof}
559	
560	\begin{remark}
561	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-$0$ Albanese keystone]
562	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}
563	  Closure of \Cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1} retires the
564	  \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
565	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}
566	  (\Cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of
567	  \Cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, body landed iter-166), whose
568	  \emph{first} of two upstream \texttt{sorry} sources is the
569	  \texttt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} target this chapter closes. The
570	  \emph{second} upstream is the helper
571	  \texttt{morphism\_P1\_to\_grpScheme\_const}'s residual sorries, tracked
572	  separately under the genus-$0$ Albanese arm of \texttt{Jacobian.tex}.
573	\end{remark}
574	
575	\section{Out of scope}
576	
577	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
578	are sequenced elsewhere:
579	
580	\begin{itemize}
581	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ of
582	    genus $0$ over a \emph{non-closed} field $k$ with a $k$-rational point
583	    $\cong \mathbb P^1_k$'' statement is the $\bar k \to k$ descent of
584	    \Cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}. The descent is hosted in
585	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
586	    \Cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1} together with Galois descent for
587	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
588	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
589	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
590	    consumed as a black box by
591	    \Cref{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
592	    Mathlib (the universal property of $\mathrm{Proj}$, graded-ring
593	    presentation of $\mathbb P^n$) is not re-derived here.
594	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
595	    is consumed as a black box by \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
596	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
597	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
598	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
599	    the same logical level as the closely related
600	    \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
601	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
602	    $\bar k$ with dominant morphisms'' $\cong$ ``function fields of
603	    transcendence degree~$1$ over $\bar k$ with $\bar k$-homomorphisms''
604	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
605	    \Cref{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
606	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
607	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
608	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
609	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
610	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
611	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$
612	    has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
613	    cardinality of $C(\bar k)$) is consumed implicitly by the present
614	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
615	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
616	    does not restate it.
617	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$ on a curve
618	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
619	    particular the $H^1$-vanishing of $\mathcal O_C([P])$ on a genus-$0$
620	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
621	    \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
622	    re-derived here.
623	\end{itemize}

2	
3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
9	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
10	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
11	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[
70	  (\ast)\qquad
71	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
72	  codimension one.}
73	\]
74	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
75	additionally assume $X$ is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
76	an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, where Hartshorne's condition $(*)$ is
77	automatic.
78	
79	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
80	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
81	$(\ast)$ as a single typeclass. The chapter pins $(*)$ to a \emph{layered}
82	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
83	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
84	
85	\begin{itemize}
86	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \Cref{def:prime_divisor} and
87	    \Cref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group $\Div(X)$, the degree
88	    \Cref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
89	    \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
90	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
91	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
92	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of $X$ already
93	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
94	    chain), so $X$ has a unique generic point and the function field
95	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
96	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
97	    the bare formal-sum data type.
98	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\Cref{def:order_at_point},
99	    \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, \Cref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
100	    signature additionally threads
101	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
102	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
103	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
104	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
105	    predicate ``for every $x \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1$ the
106	    stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
107	    the conjunction
108	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
109	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
110	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
111	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
112	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
113	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
114	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
115	    over $\bar k$ with explicit integrality witness, namely
116	    \begin{quote}
117	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
118	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
119	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
120	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
121	    \end{quote}
122	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
123	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
124	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's $(*)$ is
125	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
126	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
127	    properness; integrality threaded).
128	\end{itemize}
129	
130	\noindent
131	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
132	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
133	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
134	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
135	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
136	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
137	
138	\section{Codim-$1$ cycle group}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
145	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
147	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
148	  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
149	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
150	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
151	  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one."
152	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
153	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. A \emph{prime divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral
154	  subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
155	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
156	  Y$ be its generic point\ldots''), $Y$ is determined by its generic point
157	  $\eta \in X$: closed integral subschemes of codimension one of $X$ are in
158	  natural bijection with points $x \in X$ such that the closure
159	  $\overline{\{x\}} \subset X$ has codimension one. The closure
160	  $\overline{\{x\}}$, equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
161	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
162	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
163	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
167	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
168	  the Mathlib predicate
169	  \[
170	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
171	  \]
172	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying $X$.
173	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
174	  ``$x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x$'' (i.e.\ $x \in \overline{\{y\}}$;
175	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
176	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
177	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
178	  preorder, so for the generic point $\eta_Y$ of a prime divisor $Y$ the
179	  only point strictly above $\eta_Y$ is the generic point $\eta_X$ of $X$,
180	  and the chain $\eta_Y < \eta_X$ has length one, giving
181	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta_Y$\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
182	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
183	  integral'' is automatic from $\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}$ being
184	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
185	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
186	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
187	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
188	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
189	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
190	
191	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
192	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
193	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
194	  \begin{itemize}
195	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
196	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
197	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
198	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
199	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
200	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
201	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
202	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
203	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
204	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
205	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
206	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
207	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
208	      uses.
209	  \end{itemize}
210	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
211	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
212	  needs.
213	\end{definition}
214	
215	\begin{definition}
216	
217	\leanok
218	[Codim-$1$ cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
219	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
221	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
222	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
223	  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
224	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
225	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
226	  % group $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
227	  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
228	  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from
229	  % zero. If all the $n_i \geq 0$, we say that $D$ is \emph{effective}."
230	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
231	  Let $X$ be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
232	  codimension one (i.e.\ $X$ satisfies $(*)$). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
233	  \[
234	    \Div(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
235	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
236	  \]
237	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of $X$
238	  (\Cref{def:prime_divisor}); an element $D = \sum n_i Y_i$ with finitely
239	  many nonzero coefficients $n_i \in \mathbb Z$ is called a \emph{Weil
240	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all $n_i \geq 0$.
241	
242	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
243	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
244	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
245	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor $\to_0$ $\mathbb Z$}; integrality of $X$ is
246	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group $\Div(X)$ (it becomes
247	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
248	  field appears). Hartshorne's $(*)$ is the standing convention in the
249	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
250	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding'' above
251	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
252	\end{definition}

254	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
255	
256	\begin{definition}
257	
258	\leanok
259	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
260	  \label{def:order_at_point}
261	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
262	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
263	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
264	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
265	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
266	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
267	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
268	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
269	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
270	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
271	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
273	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
274	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
275	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
276	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}
277	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a prime divisor $Y \subset X$ with generic
278	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
279	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
280	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
281	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
282	  \[
283	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
284	  \]
285	  where $v_Y$ is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
286	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of
287	  order $\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}; if $\ord_Y(f) < 0$, $f$ has a \emph{pole
288	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.
289	
290	  Specialisation to a curve. If $C$ is a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$
291	  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
292	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
293	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
294	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
295	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.
296	
297	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
298	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
299	  \begin{quote}
300	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
301	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
302	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
303	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : $\mathbb Z$}
304	  \end{quote}
305	  with \emph{no} $f \neq 0$ hypothesis (junk-on-$f = 0$ convention,
306	  see below). The body is
307	  \[
308	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
309	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
310	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
311	      \bigr).
312	  \]
313	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
314	  \begin{itemize}
315	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K $\to\!\!*_0$ $\mathbb Z^{m_0}$}
316	      (with $\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)$),
317	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
318	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
319	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
320	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-$\leq 1$ ring to
321	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
322	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on $r \in R$ is the
323	      length of $R/(r)$ (the DVR valuation); on $a/b \in K$, it is
324	      $\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)$. The hypotheses
325	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
326	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
327	      synthesises from $[\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]$ (delivers
328	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
329	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
330	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
331	      X.functionField} via
332	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
333	      from $[\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]$ (delivers
334	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
335	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
336	      and from the explicitly threaded
337	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
338	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : $\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z$}
339	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
340	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
341	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: $\log 0 = 0$;
342	      \texttt{log\_mul}: $\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y$ for
343	      $x, y \neq 0$. This makes the downstream additive identities
344	      $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$, $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$,
345	      $v_Y(1) = 0$ available as named lemmas for
346	      \Cref{thm:principal_hom}.
347	  \end{itemize}
348	
349	  \emph{Junk-on-$f = 0$ convention.} Setting $f = 0$ in
350	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds $0$ into
351	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
352	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps $0$ to
353	  $0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}$); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
354	  $\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0$. This is the canonical Mathlib junk
355	  choice; no separate $f \neq 0$ hypothesis is threaded on the
356	  signature.
357	
358	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
359	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
360	  topological condition $\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1$
361	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
362	  $\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}$ on the
363	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
364	  the topological identity ``coheight of $x$ in the scheme's
365	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
366	  corresponding to $x$ in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
367	  ``ideal height $=$ stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
368	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
369	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
370	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
371	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
372	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
373	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
374	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
375	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
376	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
377	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
378	\end{definition}
