380	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
381	
382	\begin{definition}
383	
384	\leanok
385	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
386	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
388	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
389	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
390	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
391	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
392	  % be written $D = \sum n_i P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and
393	  % $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
394	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If $X$
395	  % is a nonsingular curve, then $X$ satisfies the condition $(\ast)$ used
396	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on $X$. A prime divisor is just
397	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
398	  % P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
399	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
400	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over a field $k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
401	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to $P$} is the element
402	  \[
403	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \Div(C)
404	  \]
405	  i.e.\ the prime divisor $P$ with multiplicity one. The assignment is
406	  well-defined because the closed point $P$ is automatically a codimension-$1$
407	  integral closed subscheme of the $1$-dimensional integral scheme $C$.
408	  Conversely, every prime divisor on $C$ is of this form, so an arbitrary
409	  divisor on $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb Z$-linear combination
410	  $\sum n_i [P_i]$ of closed points.
411	
412	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
413	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
414	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
415	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
416	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' at the
417	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
418	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
419	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
420	  codim-$1$ regime:
421	  \[
422	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
423	      \begin{cases}
424	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
425	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
426	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
427	      \end{cases}
428	  \]
429	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
430	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
431	  of codimension $\geq 2$ on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
432	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor $0 \in \Div(C)$. This
433	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
434	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
435	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
436	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
437	  \Cref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \Cref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
438	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
439	
440	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme $C$, $P$ a
441	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
442	  $[P] := 1 \cdot P \in \Div(C)$ is recovered by combining
443	  \Cref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} with the chain
444	  $\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1$
445	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
446	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
447	\end{definition}
448	
449	\begin{lemma}
450	
451	\leanok
452	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-$1$ regime]
453	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
454	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
455	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:prime_divisor}
456	  Let $C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}$ be a scheme and let $P \in C$ be a
457	  point with $\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)$. If
458	  $h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1$ (the codimension-one witness
459	  promoting $P$ to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \Cref{def:prime_divisor}),
460	  then
461	  \[
462	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
463	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
464	      \;\in\; \Div(C).
465	  \]
466	\end{lemma}
467	
468	\begin{proof}
469	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point}
470	  \leanok
471	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
472	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
473	  positive branch using the hypothesis $h$.
474	\end{proof}
475	
476	\begin{lemma}
477	
478	\leanok
479	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
480	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
481	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
482	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point}
483	  Let $C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}$ be a scheme and let $P \in C$ be a
484	  point with $\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)$. If
485	  $\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1$ (the off-branch: $P$ is not a
486	  codimension-one point of $C$ --- e.g.\ $P$ is the generic point of an
487	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
488	  scheme), then
489	  \[
490	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
491	      0 \;\in\; \Div(C).
492	  \]
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point}
497	  \leanok
498	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
499	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
500	  negative branch using the hypothesis
501	  $\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1$.
502	\end{proof}
503	
504	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
505	
506	\begin{definition}
507	
508	\leanok
509	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over $\bar k$]
510	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
512	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_closed_point}
513	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
514	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
515	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
516	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of $D$ to be $\sum n_i$."
517	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
518	  nonsingular curve).}
519	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
520	  $\bar k$. The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
521	  \Div(C)$ is the integer
522	  \[
523	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
524	  \]
525	  The definition uses that every closed point of $C$ has residue field
526	  $\bar k$ (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
527	  field $k$ the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
528	  $\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]$ recovers the
529	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
530	  $\bar k$ specialisation.
531	
532	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
533	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
534	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
535	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
536	  set ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
537	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
538	  $D \in \Div(X)$ on any scheme $X$ the integer-valued sum of its
539	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ this coincides
540	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
541	  residue field $\bar k$, so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
542	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
543	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
544	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
545	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
546	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
547	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
548	\end{definition}
549	
550	\begin{theorem}
551	
552	\leanok
553	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
554	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
556	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
557	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
558	  $\bar k$. The degree map of \Cref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
559	  homomorphism
560	  \[
561	    \deg \colon \Div(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
562	    \qquad
563	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
564	    \qquad
565	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
566	  \]
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	\begin{proof}
570	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
571	  \leanok
572	  Immediate from the definition: $\Div(C)$ is the free abelian group on the
573	  set of closed points of $C$, and $\deg$ is the unique group homomorphism
574	  sending each generator $[P]$ to $1 \in \mathbb Z$ (equivalently: the sum
575	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
576	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
577	\end{proof}
578	
579	\section{Principal divisors}
580	
581	\begin{definition}
582	
583	\leanok
584	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
585	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
587	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
588	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of $\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)$,
589	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
590	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
591	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
592	  % many prime divisors $Y$."
593	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$ and let $f \in K^\ast$.
594	  % We define the \emph{divisor of $f$}, denoted $(f)$, by
595	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime
596	  % divisors $Y$ of $X$. By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
597	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
598	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
599	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
600	  p.~131.}
601	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$,
602	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that $\ord_Y(f) = 0$ for all but finitely
603	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum
604	  \[
605	    \div(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
606	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \Div(X)
607	  \]
608	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
609	  \emph{principal divisor of $f$}. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over $\bar k$
610	  the same definition specialises to
611	  $\div(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]$, the formal
612	  sum over closed points.
613	\end{definition}
614	
615	\begin{theorem}
616	
617	\leanok
618	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
619	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
621	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
622	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
623	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
624	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
625	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
626	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
627	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
628	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
629	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
630	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
631	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
632	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
633	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
634	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
635	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
636	  \[
637	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
638	    \qquad
639	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
640	    \qquad
641	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
642	  \]
643	\end{theorem}
644	
645	\begin{proof}
646	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
647	  \leanok
648	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
649	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
650	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
651	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
652	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
653	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
654	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
655	  identities.
656	\end{proof}
657	
658	\begin{theorem}
659	
660	\leanok
661	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
662	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
663	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
664	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
665	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
666	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
667	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
668	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
669	  % nonsingular curve $X$ has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
670	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
671	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
672	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
673	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
674	  $\bar k$. For every nonzero rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ the
675	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
676	  \[
677	    \deg(\div(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
678	  \]
679	\end{theorem}
680	
681	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let $f \in K(X)^\ast$. If $f \in k$, then
682	% $(f) = 0$, so there is nothing to prove. If $f \notin k$, then the
683	% inclusion of fields $k(f) \subset K(X)$ induces a finite morphism
684	% $\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1$. It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
685	% have $(f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})$. Since $\{0\} - \{\infty\}$
686	% is a divisor of degree 0 on $\mathbf P^1$, we conclude that $(f)$ has
687	% degree 0 on $X$."
688	\begin{proof}
689	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
690	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If $f \in \bar k \subset K(C)$ is
691	  a constant scalar, then $f$ has no zeros or poles, so $\div(f) = 0$ and
692	  $\deg(\div(f)) = 0$. Otherwise the inclusion of fields
693	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ exhibits $K(C)$ as a finite extension of the
694	  rational function field $\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)$, so the corresponding
695	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is finite (by the
696	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
697	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
698	  divisor $\div(f)$ equals the pullback $\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])$
699	  along $\varphi$ of the degree-$0$ divisor $[0] - [\infty]$ on
700	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$; since pullback along a finite morphism
701	  multiplies degrees by $\deg(\varphi)$, we conclude
702	  $\deg(\div(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
703	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
704	
705	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
706	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ defines a finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$''
707	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
708	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
709	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
710	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
711	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
712	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
713	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
714	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
715	\end{proof}

717	\section{Divisor class group}
718	
719	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
720	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
721	the \emph{Picard group} of $C$ in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
722	interpretation $\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)$ requires the construction of
723	$\mathcal O_C(D)$, which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
724	out of scope here.
725	
726	\begin{definition}
727	
728	\leanok
729	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
730	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
731	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
732	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
733	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
734	  % and of $\operatorname{Cl} X$) (read from
735	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
736	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. Two divisors $D$
737	  % and $D'$ are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$,
738	  % if $D - D'$ is a principal divisor. The group $\operatorname{Div} X$ of
739	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
740	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of $X$, and is denoted by
741	  % $\operatorname{Cl} X$."
742	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
743	  $\operatorname{Cl} X$).}
744	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. Two Weil divisors $D, D' \in \Div(X)$ are
745	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$, if and only if there
746	  exists $f \in K(X)^{\times}$ with
747	  \[
748	    D - D' \;=\; \div(f) \;\in\; \Div(X).
749	  \]
750	  The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $\Div(X)$ (the
751	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
752	  identities $\div(1) = 0$, $\div(f^{-1}) = -\div(f)$, $\div(fg) =
753	  \div(f) + \div(g)$ via \Cref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
754	  quotient group $\Cl(X) := \Div(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)$ is the
755	  \emph{divisor class group} of $X$. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over
756	  $\bar k$, \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
757	  $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ descends to a homomorphism
758	  $\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z$.
759	\end{definition}

762	
763	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
764	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
765	
766	\begin{itemize}
767	  \item The line-bundle construction $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to a Weil
768	    divisor $D$, the calculation of its global sections, and the
769	    identification $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when
770	    $D \sim D'$, lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
771	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
772	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
773	    $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ and its genus-$0$ specialisation
774	    $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$ live in \texttt{RR.2}
775	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
776	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
777	    curve over $\bar k$ with $g = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$''
778	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
779	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
780	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
781	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ together
782	    with its degree-$1$ identification.
783	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
784	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
785	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
786	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
787	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
788	    $\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)$) are out of scope: this chapter is
789	    strictly curve-focused.
790	  \item The proof of \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
791	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
792	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
793	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
794	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
795	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
796	\end{itemize}

644	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
645	
646	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-$0$ base case. The two
647	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
648	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
649	G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ on $\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
650	the base case ``every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
651	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one axis of the
652	decomposition, giving $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense $\mathbb G_m$. The
653	classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive homomorphism step
654	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
655	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
656	\emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
657	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
658	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb
659	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
660	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
661	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
662	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-$0$
663	path. The genus-$0$ output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
664	(here: regular) map $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant''.
665	
666	\begin{remark}
667	  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
668	  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-$0$ path.} An earlier draft of
669	  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
670	  base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ constant''. That was a misreading. The base case
671	  (Proposition~3.9, the $d = 1$ case of Proposition~3.10) follows directly from the Rigidity
672	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
673	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
674	  action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one
675	  axis of the decomposition, yielding $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense
676	  $\mathbb G_m$ (the primary $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; see
677	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
678	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)$ (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
679	  Milne-faithful $\mathbb G_a$-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
680	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is already a
681	  total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb P^1$, no defect map needs extending over a complete
682	  surface (this corrects the earlier claim that the surface extension was on the genus-$0$ path;
683	  see \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) enters only for
684	  projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties, polarizations, and the
685	  Poincar\'e sheaf --- none on the genus-$0$ path. For Route~A: the Albanese universal property
686	  that the positive-genus arm needs (Milne Proposition~6.1/6.4, \S III.6) is \emph{also} cube-free,
687	  resting on Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) + Corollary~1.2/1.5 --- which is
688	  precisely why Theorem~3.2 and Corollary~1.2 are retained in this chapter as Route-A inputs.
689	\end{remark}
690	
691	\begin{lemma}
692	
693	\leanok
694	[Additive decomposition over a product (Milne Corollary 1.5)]
695	  \label{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity}
697	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma}
698	  % NOTE: (iter-163 review) The Lean signature carries three product-level instance
699	  % hypotheses on V⊗W not spelled out in the prose: [GeometricallyIrreducible (V⊗W).hom],
700	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (V⊗W).hom], [IsReduced (V⊗W).left] — i.e. "V×W is a variety".
701	  % These are mathematically free for genuine complete varieties V, W, but Mathlib does not
702	  % auto-derive the product instances from the factors, so they are carried explicitly to keep
703	  % the lemma axiom-clean. Also: the Lean states the EXISTENCE half only (canonical
704	  % axis-restriction witnesses inlined; uniqueness not formalized) and requires only V (not W)
705	  % complete — strictly more general than the prose. (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter163.)
706	  % NOTE: (iter-164 review) iter-164 hygiene dropped the A-side instances [Smooth A.hom] and
707	  % [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] from the Lean signature; the abelian-variety target now
708	  % requires only [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom]. The Lean signature is therefore STRICTLY MORE
709	  % GENERAL than the prose ("Let A be an abelian variety"): every situation the prose covers is
710	  % covered, and additionally any proper group object suffices. Axiom-clean preserved.
711	  % (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter164.)
712	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 15)
713	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.5. Let V and W be complete varieties over k with k-rational
714	  % points v0 and w0, and let p and q be the projection maps V×W→V and V×W→W. Let A be an
715	  % abelian variety. Then a morphism h:V×W→A such that h(v0,w0)=0 can be written uniquely as
716	  % h = f∘p + g∘q with f:V→A and g:W→A morphisms such that f(v0)=0 and g(w0)=0."
717	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
718	  Let $V$, $W$ be complete varieties over $\bar k$ with $\bar k$-points $v_0 \in V$,
719	  $w_0 \in W$, and let $p \colon V \times W \to V$, $q \colon V \times W \to W$ be the
720	  projections. Let $A$ be an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then a morphism
721	  $h \colon V \times W \to A$ with $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$ can be written uniquely as
722	  $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$, with morphisms $f \colon V \to A$, $g \colon W \to A$ satisfying
723	  $f(v_0) = 0$ and $g(w_0) = 0$.
724	
725	  \emph{Lean encoding.} In the project $A$ is a group object \texttt{[GrpObj A]} (not assumed
726	  commutative). The ``$+$'' here is the binary operation that \texttt{GrpObj} induces on each
727	  hom-set $\mathrm{Hom}(X, A)$: for $u, v \colon X \to A$, $u + v$ is the composite
728	  $X \xrightarrow{\langle u, v\rangle} A \times A \xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{mul}\;} A$ (and
729	  $f \circ p + g \circ q$ is built this way from the projections). The decomposition is therefore
730	  read \emph{multiplicatively} as $h = (f \circ p) \cdot (g \circ q)$ in the group
731	  $\mathrm{Hom}(V \times W, A)$, and does \emph{not} require $A$ commutative; commutativity of
732	  abelian varieties (Milne Cor 1.4, a separate consequence of \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom})
733	  is needed only to rewrite the product symmetrically/additively, not for the statement or its
734	  proof. The prover should infer the hom-set group operation from the \texttt{GrpObj} instance,
735	  not from a \texttt{CommGrpObj} instance.
736	\end{lemma}
737	
738	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Set f=h|V×{w0}, g=h|{v0}×W, and identify V×{w0} and {v0}×W with
739	% V and W. On points, f(v) = h(v,w0) and g(w) = h(v0,w), and so φ =df h − (f∘p + g∘q) is the
740	% map that sends (v,w) ↦ h(v,w) − h(v,w0) − h(v0,w). Thus φ(V×{w0}) = 0 = φ({v0}×W) and so the
741	% theorem shows that φ = 0." [Milne, Cor 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9--10.]
742	\begin{proof}
743	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma}
744	  \leanok
745	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
746	  Define $f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}$ and $g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}$, identifying
747	  $V \times \{w_0\} \cong V$ and $\{v_0\} \times W \cong W$; on points $f(v) = h(v, w_0)$ and
748	  $g(w) = h(v_0, w)$. Form the difference
749	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
750	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
751	  (the subtraction is in the group $A$, i.e.\ $\varphi$ is the composite of
752	  $(h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))$ with the group law of $A$). Then $\varphi$ vanishes on both
753	  axes: $\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)$ (direct computation, using
754	  $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$). In particular $\varphi$ collapses the complete axis $V \times \{w_0\}$ to
755	  the single point $0 \in A$, so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with $V$
756	  complete and $V \times W$ geometrically irreducible) $\varphi$ factors through the projection
757	  $q$; but it also vanishes on $\{v_0\} \times W$, which is a section of $q$, so the factoring
758	  morphism is identically $0$, i.e.\ $\varphi \equiv 0$. Hence $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$.
759	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers $f$ and $g$ as the
760	  axis-restrictions of $h$.
761	\end{proof}

788	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
789	  Every regular map $\alpha \colon A \to B$ of abelian varieties over $\bar k$ is the composite
790	  of a homomorphism with a translation; equivalently, if $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$ then $\alpha$ is a
791	  homomorphism, i.e.\ $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$ for all $a, a' \in A$.
792	\end{lemma}
793	
794	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The regular map α will send the k-rational point 0 of A to a
795	% k-rational point b of B. After composing α with translation by −b, we may assume that
796	% α(0)=0. Consider the map φ:A×A→B, φ(a,a′)=α(a+a′) − α(a) − α(a′). By this we mean that φ is
797	% the difference of the two regular maps A×A →(m)→ A →(α)→ B and A×A →(α×α)→ B×B →(m)→ B, which
798	% is a regular map. Then φ(A×0)=0=φ(0×A) and so φ=0. This means that α is a homomorphism."
799	% [Milne, Cor 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9.]
800	\begin{proof}
801	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
802	  \leanok
803	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
804	  The image $\alpha(0_A)$ is a $\bar k$-point $b$ of $B$; composing $\alpha$ with translation by
805	  $-b$ we may assume $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$, and it suffices to show such an $\alpha$ is a
806	  homomorphism. Form
807	  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]
808	  the difference of the two regular maps $A \times A \xrightarrow{m} A \xrightarrow{\alpha} B$
809	  and $A \times A \xrightarrow{\alpha \times \alpha} B \times B \xrightarrow{m} B$. Then
810	  $\varphi(0_A, 0_A) = 0$ and $\varphi$ vanishes on both axes,
811	  $\varphi(A \times 0) = 0 = \varphi(0 \times A)$ (since $\alpha(0) = 0$). By the additive
812	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (with $V = W = A$), the axis-restrictions
813	  of $\varphi$ being $0$ force $\varphi \equiv 0$; equivalently, $\varphi$ collapses the complete
814	  axis $A \times 0$ and vanishes on $0 \times A$, so Rigidity gives $\varphi = 0$. Hence
815	  $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$, i.e.\ $\alpha$ is a homomorphism.
816	\end{proof}

818	\begin{lemma}
819	[A rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends (Milne Theorem 3.2)]
820	  \label{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}
821	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_to_av_extends}
822	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 3.2 (with Theorem 3.1 and Lemma 3.3), \S I.3, doc p.~16--18 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 22--24)
823	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map α:V ⇢ A from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
824	  % variety is defined on the whole of V."
825	  % SOURCE QUOTE (Thm 3.1, used in the proof): "THEOREM 3.1. A rational map φ:V ⇢ W from a
826	  % normal variety V to a complete variety W is defined on an open subset U of V whose
827	  % complement V∖U has codimension ≥ 2."
828	  % SOURCE QUOTE (Lemma 3.3, used in the proof): "LEMMA 3.3. Let φ:V ⇢ G be a rational map from
829	  % a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either φ is defined on all of V or the points
830	  % where it is not defined form a closed subset of pure codimension 1 in V (i.e., a finite
831	  % union of prime divisors)."
832	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2 (with Theorem~3.1, Lemma~3.3).}
833	
834	  \textbf{Route-A-only (iter-164).} This extension lemma is \emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical
835	  path; the 4th route (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}, \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}) closes the
836	  base case without it. It is retained here only as an input to Route~A (the positive-genus
837	  Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6) and carries the route's riskiest sub-build
838	  (\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}).
839	
840	  A rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$ from a nonsingular variety $V$ over
841	  $\bar k$ to an abelian variety $A$ is defined on the whole of $V$.
842	\end{lemma}
843	
844	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma." [Milne, Thm 3.2, \S I.3,
845	% doc p.~17.]
846	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Thm 3.1, general case): "Let U be the largest subset on which φ is
847	% defined, and suppose that V∖U has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor Z in V∖U.
848	% Because V is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete valuation ring O_Z with field of
849	% fractions k(V). The map φ defines a morphism of schemes Spec(k(V)) → W, which the valuative
850	% criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a morphism Spec(O_Z) → W.
851	% This implies that φ has a representative defined on an open subset that meets Z in a nonempty
852	% set, which is a contradiction." [Milne, Thm 3.1, \S I.3, doc p.~17.]
853	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Lemma 3.3, the difference-map): "Define a rational map Φ:V×V ⇢ G,
854	% (x,y) ↦ φ(x)·φ(y)⁻¹. ... Thus φ is defined at x if and only if Φ is defined at (x,x). ...
855	% Suppose φ is not defined at x. Then for some f ∈ Im(Φ*), (x,x) ∈ div(f)₁, and clearly Φ is
856	% not defined at the points (y,y) ∈ Δ ∩ div(f)₁. This is a subset of pure codimension one in Δ,
857	% and when we identify it with a subset of V, it is a subset of V of codimension one passing
858	% through x on which φ is not defined." [Milne, Lemma 3.3, \S I.3, doc p.~17--18.]
859	\begin{proof}
860	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2.}
861	  The statement is Theorem~3.1 combined with Lemma~3.3. By Theorem~3.1 (a consequence of the
862	  valuative criterion of properness, Mathlib \texttt{IsProper}/\texttt{ValuativeCriterion},
863	  applied at the discrete valuation ring of each codimension-$1$ point), a rational map from the
864	  normal $V$ to the complete $A$ is defined outside a closed set of codimension $\geq 2$. By
865	  Lemma~3.3, the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a \emph{group} variety is either
866	  empty or pure of codimension $1$: forming the difference map
867	  $\Phi(x, y) = \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow A$, one shows
868	  $\varphi$ is defined at $x$ iff $\Phi$ is defined at the diagonal point $(x, x)$, and an
869	  indeterminacy point produces a pure-codimension-$1$ component of the non-domain. The two
870	  facts are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty; hence $\alpha$ is everywhere
871	  defined.
872	\end{proof}
873	
874	\begin{remark}
875	  \label{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}
876	  \textbf{Formalisation risk: the pure-codimension-$1$ step (Lemma 3.3) has no Mathlib
877	  Weil-divisor support.} Theorem~3.1's everywhere-extension-outside-codimension-$2$ is within
878	  reach of Mathlib's valuative criterion of properness
879	  (\texttt{ValuativeCriterion}/\texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}), applied at the
880	  discrete valuation ring $\mathcal O_{V, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$. But Lemma~3.3,
881	  which rules out codimension-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety, is proved in
882	  Milne via Weil-divisor theory ($\mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_1$, prime divisors,
883	  tangent cones) for which Mathlib currently has \emph{no} usable API at this generality. This
884	  is the route's riskiest sub-build. An open question for the prover: whether a \emph{pointwise}
885	  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ separately, that the
886	  morphism $\Spec k(V) \to A$ extends over $\Spec \mathcal O_{V, Z}$ using only that $A$ is
887	  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at $Z$ without
888	  globally building $\mathrm{div}(f)$) --- side-steps building divisor theory entirely.
889	
890	  \textbf{This surface extension is \emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path (corrected, iter-164).}
891	  An earlier draft routed the genus-$0$ base case through this extension, by collapsing the
892	  additive-defect map $\psi(x, y) = f(x + y) - f(x) - f(y)$ on the \emph{affine} surface
893	  $\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a$ and then extending $\psi$ over the complete surface
894	  $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ --- which genuinely \emph{would} need Theorem~3.2's surface case
895	  (Milne Theorem~3.4 / Lemma~3.3). That detour is unnecessary. The 4th route
896	  (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}) replaces the two-variable affine defect map by the
897	  \emph{total} translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1$
898	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): $h := \sigma \fatsemi f$ is a total morphism on $\mathbb P^1
899	  \times \mathbb G_a$ (no extension needed), and the complete-source factor is $\mathbb P^1$
900	  itself, so the additive decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} applies directly.
901	  Consequently \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Theorem~3.2), the codim-$1$ indeterminacy
902	  sub-build (Lemma~3.3), and the Weil-divisor gap discussed above are all confined to
903	  \textbf{Route~A} (the positive-genus Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6); they are
904	  retained in this chapter solely as Route-A inputs and may be deferred until Route~A consumes
905	  them. The genus-$0$ close no longer depends on any of them.
906	\end{remark}

908	\section{The genus-$0$ base objects and the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action (the primary route)}
909	
910	\begin{definition}
911	[Concrete genus-$0$ base objects: $\mathbb P^1$, $\mathbb G_a$, $\mathbb G_m$ over $\bar k$]
912	  \label{def:genus0_base_objects}
913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar}
914	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of standard objects; the mathematics is textbook (Hartshorne
915	  % I.2--3 for the projective line; the group laws on $\mathbb A^1$ are elementary). No external
916	  % % SOURCE QUOTE is needed for the Lean encoding itself.
917	  Work over $\Spec \bar k$ with $\bar k$ algebraically closed. The primary $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
918	  route (and its $\mathbb G_a$-additive alternative) needs only three concrete objects of
919	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$:
920	  \begin{itemize}
921	    \item $\mathbb P^1 = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, the projective line --- a smooth proper
922	      geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$ (Mathlib: projective space, or $\mathrm{Proj}$
923	      of the graded polynomial ring; \lean name \texttt{ProjectiveLineBar}). It carries
924	      the distinguished $\bar k$-points $0$, $1$, $\infty$ and the two standard affine charts
925	      $\mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$ (coordinate $x$) and
926	      $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0\}$ (coordinate $u = 1/x$).
927	    \item $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k}$, the affine line as the \emph{additive} group object
928	      (Mathlib: \texttt{AffineSpace}\,$\mathbb A(1; \Spec \bar k)$ with the $\mathtt{GrpObj}$
929	      additive structure $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$, identity $0$; \lean name \texttt{Ga}).
930	      As a scheme $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$, and the open
931	      immersion $\mathbb G_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb P^1$ has dense (cofinite) image.
932	    \item $\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\} = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0, \infty\}$, the
933	      \emph{multiplicative} group object (group law $(x, y) \mapsto xy$, identity $1$; \lean name
934	      \texttt{Gm}). Its open immersion into $\mathbb P^1$ also has dense image.
935	  \end{itemize}
936	  All three are of finite type over $\bar k$; $\mathbb P^1$ is proper, while $\mathbb G_a$ and
937	  $\mathbb G_m$ are affine (hence not complete). These are the only ``genus-$0$'' geometric inputs
938	  the base case needs.
939	\end{definition}

1049	\subsection*{Chart cover and chart-ring iso (formalisation infrastructure)}
1050	
1051	The next three declarations are iter-168 formalisation infrastructure that the chartwise
1052	construction of the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
1053	and its downstream consumers (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}) ultimately rest on.
1054	They realise, at the Lean level, the standard ``affine chart of $\mathbb P^1$ is $\mathbb
1055	A^1$'' picture: a two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ by $D_+(X_0)$ and
1056	$D_+(X_1)$; the identification of each chart ring with the one-variable polynomial ring
1057	$\bar k[u]$; and the resulting reducedness of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ as a scheme. None
1058	of them is on the genus-$0$ critical path conceptually, but each is consumed by the Lean
1059	formalisation downstream.
1060	
1061	\begin{definition}
1062	[Two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$]
1063	  \label{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
1064	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBarAffineCover}
1065	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects}

1068	  % Mathlib's $\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}$. No external % SOURCE QUOTE
1069	  % needed for the Lean encoding itself.
1070	  The two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ by the standard $D_+(X_0)$ and
1071	  $D_+(X_1)$, built by specialising Mathlib's
1072	  $\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}$ to the index set
1073	  $\iota = \{0, 1\} = \mathrm{Fin}\,2$ with family $f = (X_0, X_1)$ and weights
1074	  $m = (1, 1)$. The non-trivial input is that the irrelevant ideal of the bi-graded
1075	  $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ is contained in $\mathrm{Ideal.span}\{X_0, X_1\}$, which holds by
1076	  writing any irrelevant element as a sum of monomials of strictly positive total degree
1077	  (so each monomial is divisible by $X_0$ or $X_1$). This cover packages the two affine
1078	  charts into the data that Mathlib's $\mathrm{IsReduced.of\_openCover}$ and
1079	  $\mathrm{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}$ APIs consume, and is the chartwise glue for the
1080	  iter-169 attack on the body of $\sigma_\times = \mathtt{gmScalingP1}$
1081	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}).

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set$ of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	$C/k$ projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	$\Hilb_{C/k}$ and its open subscheme $\Div_{C/k}$ of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective $k$-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for $\Pic$ assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$. The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on $C \times_k T$ to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor $D \subset C \times_k T$ over $T$
64	(an $S$-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, hence a $T$-point of the Hilbert
66	functor $\Hilb_{C/k}(T)$, which is the special case $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of $\IHilb_{X/S}$); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat.  Then $\Div_{X/S}$ is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme $\IDiv_{X/S}$ of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  $\IHilb_{X/S}$.
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      $$A_{X/S}(T)\:\Div_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)$$
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ to the
92	  %  sheaf $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)$.  The target $\Pic_{X/S}$ may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where $\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}$).}
97	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  $T$ be a $k$-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \Div_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where $\Div_{C/k}(T)$ is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor $\Div_{C/k}$ is representable
107	  by an open subscheme $\Div_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}$ of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with $\Hilb_{C/k}$ the special case
109	  $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of $k$-schemes $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set $H:=\IHilb_{X/S}$, and let $W\subset X\x H$ be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and $q\:W\to H$ the projection.  Let $V$ denote the
118	% set of points $w\in W$ at which $W$ is an effective divisor. Plainly $V$
119	% is open in $W$.  Set $Z:=q(W-V)$.  Then $Z$ is closed because $q$ is
120	% proper.  Set $U:=H-Z$.  Then $U$ is open, and $q^{-1}U$ is an effective
121	% divisor in $X\x U$.  In fact, since $q$ is flat, $q^{-1}U$ is a relative
122	% effective divisor in $X\x U/U$.
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ is representable by a $k$-scheme; this uses
128	  $C/k$ projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let $W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}$ be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and $q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}$ the projection. The locus
131	  $V \subseteq W$ where $W$ is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement $W \setminus V$
133	  is closed in $W$, and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}$ is closed because $q$ is proper. Hence $\Div_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z$ is open in $\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on $\Div_{C/k}$ the
137	  restriction of $W$ is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \Div_{C/k} / \Div_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$
143	  to the invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in $T$: pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along $\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$
147	  takes the ideal of $D$ to the ideal of $D_{T'}$ (since $D$ is flat over
148	  $T$, the schematic inverse image $D_{T'}$ is a relative effective
149	  divisor on $C \times_k T'/T'$, again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})$.
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map $A_{C/k}(T) : \Div_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
156	\end{proof}

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: $S = \Spec k$, $X = C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	$X/S$ projective Zariski-locally over $S$, flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence,
173	        thm:quot_representable, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
174	        thm:pullback_natural}
175	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
176	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to $S = \Spec k$, $X$ complete);
177	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
178	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
179	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
180	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
181	  %   Assume $f\:X\to S$ is projective Zariski locally over $S$, and is flat
182	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
183	  %
184	  %  \tu{(1)} Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, is separated and locally of finite
185	  %  type over $S$, and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.
186	  %
187	  %  \tu{(2)} If also $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective, then
188	  %  $\IPic_{X/S}$ is a disjoint union of open
189	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
190	  %  $S$-schemes.
191	  %   \end{thm}"
192	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
193	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
194	  %    Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$, and $X$ is complete.  Then
195	  %  $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists, represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$, and is a disjoint
196	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
197	  %   \end{sbscor}"
198	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
199	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
200	  Let $k$ be a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
201	  over $k$. Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
202	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
203	  representable by a $k$-scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, separated and locally of
204	  finite type over $k$, which is a disjoint union of open
205	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
206	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ is called the
207	  \emph{Picard scheme} of $C/k$.
208	\end{theorem}
209	
210	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
211	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
212	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
213	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
214	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on $S$ to
215	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of $S$-schemes.  Moreover, it
216	% is also a local matter on $S$ to prove that an $S$-scheme is separated
217	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
218	% $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective.
219	% [\dots]
220	% Plainly an $S$-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
221	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
222	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
223	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
224	\begin{proof}
225	  \uses{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence, thm:quot_representable,
226	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:pullback_natural,
227	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
228	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to $S = \Spec k$ and
229	  $X = C$. The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
230	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
231	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
232	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
233	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
234	
235	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
236	  For each polynomial $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$, let $P^\phi \subseteq
237	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ be the \'etale subsheaf whose $T$-points are
238	  represented by invertible sheaves $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ with
239	  $\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)$ for all $t \in T$ (the
240	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
241	  $\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on the projective curve $C$). The $P^\phi$ form a
242	  disjoint open cover of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (openness via Kleiman's
243	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
244	  represent each $P^\phi$ by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
245	  $k$-schemes.
246	
247	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by $m$-regularity.}
248	  Given $\phi$, fix $m$ large enough that the $T$-points of $P^\phi$ are
249	  representable by $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ satisfying
250	  $R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0$ for all $i \ge 1$, $n \ge m$. Twisting
251	  by $\mathcal{O}_C(m)$ defines an automorphism of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
252	  that carries $P^\phi$ to $P^{\phi_0}_0$ for $\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)$.
253	  Hence it suffices to represent each $P^{\phi_0}_0$ by a quasi-projective
254	  $k$-scheme.
255	
256	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
257	  Consider the Abel map $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
258	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source $\Div_{C/k}$
259	  is representable by an open subscheme of $\Hilb_{C/k}$ (and hence by an
260	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
261	  is quasi-projective over $k$. Pulling back along
262	  $P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ yields an open
263	  subscheme $Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \Div_{C/k}$
264	  of $\Div_{C/k}$, which is again quasi-projective. The projection
265	  $\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
266	  given a $T$-point $\lambda$ of $P^{\phi_0}_0$ represented by an \'etale
267	  cover $T' \to T$ and an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}'$ on $C \times_k
268	  T'$ with $H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
269	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'$ associated to
270	  $\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'$ is smooth over $T'$; an \'etale
271	  cover $T_1 \to T'$ admits a $T'$-map $T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})$
272	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
273	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z$ produces the required $T_1$-point of $Z$.
274	  Moreover, the first projection $Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z$ is
275	  smooth and proper.
276	
277	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
278	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection $\alpha : Z \to P$
279	  of \'etale sheaves with $Z$ quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$
280	  representable, and the first projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper,
281	  $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
282	  $P = P^{\phi_0}_0$, $Z$ and $R$ as above, so $P^{\phi_0}_0$ is
283	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$ and
284	  over $m$, the disjoint union of representable $P^\phi$ pieces represents
285	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
286	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
287	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the $S = \Spec k$,
288	  $X = C$ complete case.
289	\end{proof}
290	
291	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
292	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
293	
294	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
295	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
296	abstract content allowing the descent from $Z = \Div^{\phi_0}_0$ to the
297	representing Picard piece.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}\leanok
300	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
303	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
304	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
305	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
306	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
307	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
308	  %   Let $\alpha\:Z\to P$ be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set $R:=Z\x_PZ$.
309	  %  Assume $\alpha$ is a surjection, $Z$ is representable by a
310	  %  quasi-projective $S$-scheme, $R$ is representable by an $S$-scheme, and
311	  %  the first projection $R\to Z$ is representable by a smooth and proper
312	  %  map.  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $S$-scheme, and
313	  %  $\alpha$ is representable by a smooth map."
314	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
315	  Let $\alpha : Z \to P$ be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)$.
316	  Set $R := Z \times_P Z$. Suppose:
317	  \begin{enumerate}
318	    \item $\alpha$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
319	    \item $Z$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme;
320	    \item $R$ is representable by a $k$-scheme;
321	    \item the first projection $R \to Z$ is representable by a smooth
322	      and proper map.
323	  \end{enumerate}
324	  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme, and $\alpha$
325	  is representable by a smooth map.
326	\end{lemma}
327	
328	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
329	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
330	% structural heart of step 4.)
331	% "To lighten the notation, let $Z$ and $R$ denote the
332	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map $Z\to S$ is
333	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
334	% $Z\x_SZ\to Z$ is too.  But the first projection $R\to Z$ is proper, and
335	% factors naturally through a map $h\:R\to Z\x_SZ$.  Hence $h$ is proper.
336	% But $h$ is a monomorphism; that is, $h$ is injective on $T$-points.
337	% Therefore, $h$ is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
338	%
339	% Plainly, for each $S$-scheme $T$, the subset $R(T)\subset
340	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
341	% map of schemes $R\to Z$ is flat and proper, and $Z$ is a
342	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
343	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat and projective map
344	% $Z\to Q$ such that $R=Z\x_QZ$.  (In other words, a flat and proper
345	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
346	% [\dots]
347	% Since $Z\to Q$ is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
348	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
349	% same as those of $R\to Z$.  And $R\to Z$ is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
350	% $Z\to Q$ is smooth.
351	%
352	% It remains to see that $Q$ represents $P$.  First, observe that $Z\to Q$
353	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
354	% [\dots]
355	% Since $Z\to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, $Q$ is, in the
356	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
357	% $R\rightrightarrows Z$ by Exercise~\ref{ex:epi} below, which is an
358	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
359	% exercise, $P$ too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
360	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus $Q$
361	% represents $P$."
362	\begin{proof}
363	  By (ii) the structure map $Z \to \Spec k$ is quasi-projective, hence
364	  separated, so the first projection $Z \times_k Z \to Z$ is separated.
365	  By (iv) the first projection $R \to Z$ is proper; it factors through
366	  $h : R \to Z \times_k Z$ over the diagonal embedding (the second
367	  component is the second projection of $R \to Z \times_P Z$). The map
368	  $h$ is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because $R$ is the graph
369	  of an equivalence relation on $Z$, hence injective on $T$-points).
370	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: $h$ is a
371	  closed embedding.
372	
373	  Hence $R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z$ is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
374	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$. By the
375	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
376	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
377	  the graph $R \subseteq Z \times_k Z$ inside the universal subscheme of
378	  the Hilbert scheme $\Hilb_{Z/k}$ and descends to a closed subscheme
379	  $Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}$ representing the quotient), there exist a
380	  quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat, projective map
381	  $Z \to Q$ with $R = Z \times_Q Z$.
382	
383	  The map $Z \to Q$ is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
384	  extension, coincide with those of $R \to Z$, which are smooth by
385	  hypothesis. Hence $Z \to Q$ is smooth.
386	
387	  Finally, $Z \to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
388	  cover of any $T$-point of $Q$ pulls back to a $T'$-point of $Z$, by
389	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to $Z \times_Q T \to T$). In the category
390	  of \'etale sheaves, both $Q$ and $P$ are coequalisers of the pair
391	  $R \rightrightarrows Z$; the coequaliser is unique up to unique
392	  isomorphism, so $Q$ represents $P$. Smoothness of $\alpha : Z \to P$
393	  is the smoothness of $Z \to Q$ just shown.
394	\end{proof}
395	
396	\section{Group-scheme structure}
397	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
398	
399	The Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ inherits an abelian group structure from
400	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on $C \times_k T$. The relative
401	Picard presheaf $T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T$ is already an abelian
402	group at every $T$ (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
403	functorially in $T$; this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
404	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
405	
406	\begin{theorem}\leanok
407	[$\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group scheme]
408	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
409	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
410	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, thm:fga_pic_representability,
411	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
412	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
413	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
414	  % text (group-scheme structure on $\IPic_{X/S}$ via tensor product);
415	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
416	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
418	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
419	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
420	  %      $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
421	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
422	  %  topologies  by
423	  %      $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
424	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
425	  %   \end{dfn}"
426	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
427	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
428	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
429	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
430	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
431	  %  Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists."
432	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
433	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
434	  Let $\Pic_{C/k}$ be the Picard scheme of
435	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then $\Pic_{C/k}$ is canonically an
436	  abelian group scheme over $k$: the tensor product
437	  $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]$
438	  and inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$ on
439	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ (well defined because both operations
440	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
441	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
442	  are natural in $T$, hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
443	  $m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$,
444	  $i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$, and
445	  $e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}$ (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
446	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, $\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group
447	  scheme locally of finite type.
448	\end{theorem}
449	
450	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
451	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
452	% the fact that $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
453	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
454	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
455	% records the standard transfer.
456	\begin{proof}
457	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
458	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
459	  The relative Picard functor $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ of
460	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
461	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
462	  \mathcal{M}]$, inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
463	  $0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]$; these operations descend through
464	  the quotient by $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ because $\pi_T^*$ is itself a
465	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
466	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
467	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
468	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
469	  in the value category).
470	
471	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ is
472	  represented by the scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$. The Yoneda embedding is fully
473	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
474	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
475	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
476	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (inverse), and the
477	  unit map $h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ pick out unique
478	  morphisms of representing schemes $m$, $i$, $e$ as displayed. The
479	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
480	  inverse) hold at the level of $T$-points functorially in $T$, hence
481	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
482	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
483	\end{proof}
484	
485	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
486	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
487	
488	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
489	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
490	
491	\begin{definition}\leanok
492	[The Picard scheme]
493	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
494	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
495	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
496	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
497	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
498	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
499	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
500	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
501	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
502	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
503	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
504	  %  Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists."
505	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
506	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
507	  $k$. The \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$ is the $k$-scheme representing
508	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$, supplied by
509	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
510	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
511	  locally of finite type over $k$, and a disjoint union of open
512	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes.
513	\end{definition}

546	    \times_k Z$ on a quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$ to a

578	\section{Out of scope}
579	\label{sec:fga_pic_out_of_scope}
580	
581	This chapter packages \emph{only} the representable Picard scheme
582	$\Pic_{C/k}$ as an assembly of sibling-chapter inputs, plus the
583	group-scheme refinement. The following downstream constructions live in
584	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
585	\begin{itemize}
586	  \item \textbf{Identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$} (A.3) --- the open and
587	    closed subgroup scheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$ containing the unit and
588	    consisting of those classes lying in the connected component of
589	    $\Pic_{C/k}$. Its dimension, smoothness, and abelian-variety
590	    structure are the content of the A.3 chapter
591	    \texttt{Picard\_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex} (planned), gated on this
592	    chapter.
593	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme representability} --- consumed by
594	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} via \texttt{thm:quot\_representable}
595	    (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), not re-proved here.
596	  \item \textbf{\'Etale sheafification of $\Pic^\sharp$} ---
597	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c) produces
598	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ from the set-valued presheaf of
599	    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}; this chapter consumes the
600	    sheaf as a black box.
601	  \item \textbf{Universal Poincar\'e sheaf} (Kleiman~\S 4 Ex.~ex:univshf)
602	    --- exists when $\Pic_{C/k}$ represents $\Pic_{C/k}$ (as opposed to
603	    its \'etale sheafification) and $C/k$ has a section; out of scope
604	    here. The Albanese / Abel--Jacobi consumption in
605	    \cref{chap:AbelJacobi} works with the \'etale-sheafified
606	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ representation and does not require the
607	    Poincar\'e sheaf.
608	  \item \textbf{Mumford's example} (Kleiman~\S 4 eg:Mumford), counter-examples
609	    to representability for non-integral geometric fibers --- not
610	    relevant to a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
611	\end{itemize}

613	\section{Internal-consistency check}
614	\label{sec:fga_pic_consistency_check}
615	
616	The five declaration blocks form a connected $\uses$ chain rooted in the
617	sibling chapters \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback},

1251	\leanok
1252	[$\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ is reduced]
1253	  \label{lem:projlinebar_isReduced}
1254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBar_isReduced}
1255	  \uses{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
1256	  % Archon-original Lean realisation of an elementary textbook fact (projective space over
1257	  % a field is reduced) via the explicit chart cover of \cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
1258	  % and the canonical $\mathrm{val}$-injection of a homogeneous localization into the
1259	  % corresponding plain localization. No external % SOURCE QUOTE needed.
1260	  The scheme underlying $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ is reduced. By the two-chart affine cover
1261	  (\cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}) it suffices to show that each chart ring is
1262	  reduced. The chart-$i$ ring $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ embeds into the ordinary
1263	  localization $\bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]$ of the polynomial ring $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ at
1264	  the powers of $X_i$ via the canonical injection $\mathrm{val} \colon
1265	  \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \hookrightarrow \bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]$
1266	  ($\mathtt{HomogeneousLocalization.val\_injective}$); the codomain is a localization of a
1267	  polynomial ring at a non-zero-divisor (the powers of $X_i$), hence a domain, hence
1268	  reduced. A subring of a reduced ring is reduced
1269	  ($\mathtt{Function.Injective.isDomain}$ on the $\mathrm{val}$-embedding), so each chart
1270	  ring is a domain --- in particular reduced --- and the global reducedness follows.
1271	\end{lemma}

1300	  \textbf{The scaling action (primary).} The \emph{multiplicative scaling action}
1301	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1302	     \text{``}\sigma_\times(x, \lambda) = \lambda x\text{''} \]
1303	  (\lean name \texttt{gmScalingP1}) is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of
1304	  $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ --- no indeterminacy, characteristic-free (M\"obius scaling
1305	  fixing both $0$ and $\infty$) --- defined chartwise:
1306	  \begin{itemize}
1307	    \item On $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ (target chart $x$): $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$,
1308	      a polynomial map, regular everywhere.
1309	    \item Near $\infty$ (target chart $u = 1/x$): the target coordinate $1/(\lambda x)$ rewrites as
1310	      $u/\lambda$, regular everywhere on this chart because $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$ is invertible.
1311	  \end{itemize}
1312	  The two target charts cover the image and overlap consistently, so $\sigma_\times$ is defined on
1313	  the whole surface. It is a left action of the group $\mathbb G_m$ on $\mathbb P^1$:
1314	  $\sigma_\times(x, 1) = x$ and $\sigma_\times(\sigma_\times(x, \lambda), \mu) = \sigma_\times(x,
1315	  \lambda\mu)$. Crucially it has \emph{two fixed points}, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ and
1316	  $\sigma_\times(\infty, \lambda) = \infty$ for all $\lambda$; the fixed point $0$ is what makes the
1317	  $W$-axis of the additive decomposition collapse in the primary base-case proof
1318	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}).
1319	
1320	  \medskip
1321	  \noindent\textbf{Companion: the $\mathbb G_a$-translation action (demoted route only).} For the
1322	  Milne-faithful additive alternative we also record the \emph{translation action}
1323	  \[ \sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1324	     \text{``}\sigma(x, y) = x + y\text{''} \]
1325	  (\lean name \texttt{gaTranslationP1} [expected]), likewise a \emph{total} scheme morphism on all
1326	  of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a$, defined chartwise: on $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_a$
1327	  (chart $x$) it is the polynomial map $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$; near $\infty$ ($u = 1/x$) the
1328	  coordinate $1/(x + y)$ rewrites as $u/(1 + u y)$, regular at $u = 0$ (value $0$ there --- $\sigma$
1329	  fixes $\infty$ for every $y$), while the locus $x + y = 0$ is covered by the chart $x$ on which it
1330	  is the finite value $0$. It is a left action of $\mathbb G_a$ on $\mathbb P^1$ fixing $\infty$:
1331	  $\sigma(x, 0) = x$ and $\sigma(\sigma(x, y), y') = \sigma(x, y + y')$. This companion underlies
1332	  only the alternative proof of \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} and the demoted $\mathbb
1333	  G_a$-additive close of the base case (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}); it is \emph{not} on the
1334	  genus-$0$ critical path.
1335	\end{definition}

1486	  \noindent\textbf{Three-step reduction.} The proof has three steps. All three route through

1488	  ($\mathtt{Genus0BaseObjects.lean}$, post-iter-175 split moves to

1492	  $X_i$.

1495	      chart-bridge $\mathtt{awayι\_comp\_PLB\_hom}$ to rewrite the tail of the chart-$i$
