1602	\begin{lemma}
1603	[$\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$: a homomorphism from the additive group into an abelian variety is trivial]
1604	  \label{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1605	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1606	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1607	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1608	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
1609	  % NOTE (iter-164): the verbatim Milne Prop 3.9 statement+proof quotes are the prior-session-verified
1610	  % copies also carried on \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} below. This host lacks a PDF renderer
1611	  % (pdftoppm) so the quotes were NOT re-rendered from references/abelian-varieties.pdf this session;
1612	  % they are reproduced from the verified in-tree copy. See the writer report.
1613	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1614	
1615	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164).} This $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
1616	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}
1617	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
1618	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb
1619	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility) argument feeding the alternative route
1620	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-$0$ critical path depends on it.
1621	
1622	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let
1623	  $\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ be a homomorphism of group objects (so $\varphi(0) = 0_A$ and
1624	  $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$). Then $\varphi$ is the trivial homomorphism: it
1625	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1626	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1627	
1628	  \medskip
1629	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1630	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1631	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1632	\end{lemma}
1633	
1634	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1635	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1636	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1637	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1638	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1639	\begin{proof}
1640	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1641	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1642	  Write $A$ additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1643	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1644	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, which applies whenever $\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1645	  $\mathbb P^1$ (as it does in the genus-$0$ application).
1646	
1647	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is a connected subgroup of $A$
1648	  (image of the connected $\mathbb G_a$ under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1649	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of $A$, hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1650	  complete $A$); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} $\mathbb G_a$ it is moreover
1651	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over $\bar k$ is a
1652	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over $\bar k$, hence equal
1653	  to $\bar k$ (the $\Gamma = \bar k$ collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1654	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is one point;
1655	  being a homomorphism, $\varphi$ sends $0$ there, so the point is $0_A$ and
1656	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1657	
1658	  \medskip
1659	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility (when $\varphi$
1660	  extends to $\mathbb P^1$).} Suppose $\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ for a morphism
1661	  $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ (the genus-$0$ situation). Set $g := f - f(1)$, so $g(1) = 0_A$, and
1662	  form $h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A$ with the
1663	  scaling companion $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1664	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (complete),
1665	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $1 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so
1666	  $h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A$), the axis restrictions are $x \mapsto g(x)$ and
1667	  $\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)$, whence $g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)$ on $\bar k^\times$;
1668	  re-expanding $g$ gives the multiplicative relation
1669	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$ with $c = -\varphi(1)$. Now $\varphi$ is at once
1670	  additive, $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$, and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1671	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$. Computing $\varphi((x + x')\,y)$ in two ways for
1672	  $x, x' \in \bar k$, $y \in \bar k^\times$,
1673	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1674	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1675	  \varphi(y) + c$; equating gives $\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)$ for every $y \in \bar k^\times$.
1676	  Hence $\varphi$ is constant on the dense $\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a$, so constant on
1677	  $\mathbb G_a$ (density, $A$ separated), with value $\varphi(0) = 0_A$. This is Milne's argument
1678	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1679	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1680	\end{proof}
1681	
1682	\begin{remark}
1683	  \label{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}
1684	  \textbf{The primary route is the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; why no Theorem~3.2 and no
1685	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ are needed.} The genus-$0$ base case ``$f \colon \mathbb P^1
1686	  \to A$ is constant'' is closed \emph{directly} by the proven additive decomposition
1687	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} composed with a total $\mathbb G$-action on $\mathbb P^1$,
1688	  with \emph{no} appeal to Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension), the theorem of

1689	  the cube, Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials. The reason the earlier draft thought
1690	  Theorem~3.2 was needed --- that the additive defect $\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)$ lives only
1691	  on the affine $\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a$ and must be extended over $\mathbb P^1 \times
1692	  \mathbb P^1$ --- evaporates once one composes with a \emph{total} action morphism of
1693	  \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}: the composite is already a total morphism out of $\mathbb P^1 \times
1694	  \mathbb G$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$, exactly what
1695	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (whose Lean signature needs only the first factor
1696	  complete) consumes.
1697	
1698	  \medskip
1699	  \noindent\textbf{Primary route --- the scaling shortcut (strategy decision of iter-164).} The
1700	  \emph{multiplicative} scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
1701	  \mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}), closes the base case
1702	  in one shot, \emph{without} the additive homomorphism step or \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} at
1703	  all. Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ (translate; here $0$ is a scaling fixed point). Form $h_\times :=
1704	  \sigma_\times \fatsemi f$ and apply \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$,
1705	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so $h_\times(0, 1) =
1706	  f(0) = 0_A$). Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of scaling, the $W$-axis restriction collapses:
1707	  $\lambda \mapsto h_\times(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$. The decomposition
1708	  therefore reads $h_\times = p \fatsemi f$ with the second summand the group identity, i.e.
1709	  \[ \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A,
1710	     \qquad\text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''}. \]
1711	  Precomposing with $\lambda \mapsto (1, \lambda)$ gives $f|_{\mathbb G_m} = \text{const}\,f(1)$;
1712	  since $\mathbb G_m$ is dense in $\mathbb P^1$ and $A$ is separated, $f$ is constant on $\mathbb
1713	  P^1$ (the dominant-source/separated-target handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}, i.e.\ the
1714	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ family already used by \texttt{rigidity\_core}). This uses
1715	  \emph{only} the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, the total morphism $\sigma_\times$,
1716	  and density --- no $\mathbb G_a$ additivity, no \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and in
1717	  particular \emph{no} ``image of a group homomorphism is a closed subgroup'' input (a deep Mathlib
1718	  gap). It is the least-Mathlib-blocked realisation of the base case and is the route the live
1719	  consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} formalises.
1720	
1721	  \medskip
1722	  \noindent\textbf{Retained alternative --- the $\mathbb G_a$-additive route.} The Milne-faithful
1723	  argument routes through the translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to
1724	  \mathbb P^1$: $\sigma \fatsemi f$ is total, the additive decomposition makes $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ a
1725	  homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$ (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}), and a homomorphism from the
1726	  additive group into an abelian variety is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, Milne's
1727	  $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, Proposition~3.9). This reaches the same conclusion but
1728	  needs the triviality lemma; it is retained for fidelity to Milne and is \emph{off} the genus-$0$
1729	  critical path.
1730	\end{remark}

1732	\begin{lemma}
1733	[A pointed $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (Milne Prop 3.9, via the 4th route)]
1734	  \label{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}
1735	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_Ga_to_av_const}
1736	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1737	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1738	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
1739	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1740	
1741	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164): retained alternative route.} This
1742	  $\mathbb G_a$-additive close of the base case is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
1743	  shortcut} now installed as the proof of \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (see
1744	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). It is retained as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9
1745	  argument (faithful to the $\mathbb G_a \to A$ homomorphism / $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
1746	  presentation), but the live consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} no longer depends on
1747	  it; it is provided here only for completeness.
1748	
1749	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over $\bar k$ and $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ a
1750	  morphism with $f(0) = 0_A$ (the additive-identity $\bar k$-point of $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1
1751	  \subset \mathbb P^1$; any base point reduces to this by a normalising translation in $A$).
1752	  Then $f$ is constant. Concretely: the translation action $\sigma$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
1753	  makes $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ an additive-group homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$, and a homomorphism
1754	  $\mathbb G_a \to A$ is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), so $f$ collapses to the
1755	  constant $0_A$.
1756	\end{lemma}
1757	
1758	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1759	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1760	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1761	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1762	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1763	\begin{proof}
1764	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1765	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1766	  Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ by a translation in $A$ (un-translate at the end). The proof is the
1767	  \emph{4th route} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the additive defect map is never built on
1768	  the affine surface and never extended; instead we precompose $f$ with the \emph{total}
1769	  translation action $\sigma$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$ itself, so the proven
1770	  additive decomposition applies directly. Milne's Theorem~3.2
1771	  (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is \emph{not} used.
1772	
1773	  \textbf{$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is an additive homomorphism, via the total action $\sigma$.} Form
1774	  \[ h := \sigma \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to A, \qquad
1775	     \text{``}h(x, y) = f(x + y)\text{''}, \]
1776	  using the translation action $\sigma$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}; this is a \emph{total}
1777	  morphism (no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1778	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1779	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_a$, and base
1780	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $0 \in \mathbb G_a$, noting $h(0, 0) = f(0) = 0_A$. The axis
1781	  restrictions are $x \mapsto h(x, 0) = f(x)$ and $y \mapsto h(0, y) = f(y)$, so the decomposition
1782	  $h = (p \fatsemi f) + (q \fatsemi (f|_{\mathbb G_a}))$ reads, on $\bar k$-points,
1783	  \[ f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y), \qquad x \in \mathbb P^1(\bar k),\ y \in \mathbb G_a(\bar k) = \bar k. \]
1784	  Restricting $x$ to $\mathbb G_a$ shows $f|_{\mathbb G_a} \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ is a
1785	  homomorphism of group objects (it sends $0 \mapsto 0_A$ and respects addition).
1786	
1787	  \textbf{A homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$ is trivial.} By \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1788	  (the $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ lemma, the content of Milne's $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$
1789	  incompatibility), $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is the constant morphism at $0_A$. Hence $f$ is constant on
1790	  the dense open $\mathbb G_a \subset \mathbb P^1$; since $\mathbb G_a$ is dense and $A$ is
1791	  separated, $f$ is constant on all of $\mathbb P^1$, with value $f(0) = 0_A$ (i.e.\ the original
1792	  base value before the normalising translation). \qedhere
1793	
1794	  \medskip
1795	  \noindent\emph{Note: this route is the demoted alternative.} The primary base-case proof is the
1796	  $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut now installed directly as the proof of
1797	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the multiplicative
1798	  scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} gives $\sigma_\times \fatsemi f =
1799	  \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f$ (the $W$-axis collapses because $0$ is a scaling fixed point), so
1800	  $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant and $f$ is constant by density --- bypassing both the $\mathbb
1801	  G_a$-additivity step and \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and avoiding the ``image is a closed
1802	  subgroup'' Mathlib gap. The present $\mathbb G_a$-additive lemma is retained only for fidelity to
1803	  Milne; it is not on the genus-$0$ critical path.
1804	\end{proof}

1806	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
1807	
1808	\begin{proposition}
1809	
1810	\leanok
1811	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
1812	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
1813	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
1814	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
1815	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
1816	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
1817	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
1818	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
1819	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
1820	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
1821	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
1822	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
1823	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
1824	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
1825	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
1826	  single $\bar k$-point.
1827	\end{proposition}
1828	
1829	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
1830	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
1831	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
1832	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
1833	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
1834	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
1835	\begin{proof}
1836	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
1837	  \leanok
1838	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
1839	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We give the project's \textbf{primary} proof, the
1840	  \emph{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}: it proves constancy \emph{in one shot} from the proven
1841	  additive decomposition (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and a scaling fixed point,
1842	  without the intermediate ``$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ additive'' step and \emph{without} the
1843	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ triviality lemma. The theorem of the cube is \emph{not} used
1844	  (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}), and the map $f$ is already a morphism out of the complete
1845	  nonsingular $\mathbb P^1$, so Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is
1846	  \emph{not} invoked either.
1847	
1848	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Choose the scaling fixed point $0 \in \mathbb G_a \subset
1849	  \mathbb P^1$ (one of the two fixed points of $\sigma_\times$). Translating in the group $A$ by
1850	  $-f(0)$, we may assume $f(0) = 0_A$; un-translating at the end restores the original value.
1851	
1852	  \textbf{The scaling shortcut.} Form the \emph{total} morphism
1853	  \[ h := \sigma_\times \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A, \qquad
1854	     \text{``}h(x, \lambda) = f(\lambda x)\text{''}, \]
1855	  using the multiplicative scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} (a genuine
1856	  total morphism --- no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1857	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1858	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_m$, and base
1859	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$; here $h(0, 1) = f(1 \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$, as
1860	  required. The decomposition writes $h = (p \fatsemi f_0) + (q \fatsemi g)$ with $f_0$ the
1861	  $V$-axis restriction $x \mapsto h(x, 1) = f(1 \cdot x) = f(x)$ (so $f_0 = f$) and $g$ the
1862	  $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$.
1863	
1864	  \textbf{The $W$-axis collapses (the load-bearing scaling fixed point).} Because $0$ is a
1865	  \emph{fixed point} of scaling, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ for all $\lambda$
1866	  (\cref{lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero}), so the $W$-axis
1867	  restriction $g$ is the \emph{constant} morphism at $0_A$ --- the identity of the hom-group
1868	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_m, A)$. The corresponding summand $q \fatsemi g$ is therefore the group
1869	  identity, and the decomposition collapses to
1870	  \[ h = p \fatsemi f, \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
1871	     \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f, \qquad
1872	     \text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''} \quad (x \in \mathbb P^1,\ \lambda \in \mathbb G_m). \]
1873	
1874	  \textbf{Constancy via density.} Specialise $x = 1$ (precompose with $\lambda \mapsto (1,
1875	  \lambda)$): $f(\lambda) = f(1)$ for all $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$, i.e.\ $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is
1876	  the constant morphism at $f(1)$. Since $\mathbb G_m$ is dense in the irreducible $\mathbb P^1$
1877	  and $A$ is separated, the two morphisms $f$ and $\mathrm{const}_{f(1)}$, agreeing on the
1878	  non-empty open $\mathbb G_m$, agree everywhere by the dominant-source/separated-target rigidity
1879	  handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (the same \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant} family
1880	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} uses). Hence $f$ is constant, with value $f(1)$; in the normalised
1881	  picture $f(0) = 0_A$, so the constant is $0_A$ (the original $f(0)$ before translation).
1882	
1883	  \medskip
1884	  \noindent\emph{Multi-factor remark.} For the general unirational statement of Proposition~3.10,
1885	  one writes $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition
1886	  (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and applies this base case to each $\beta_i \colon
1887	  \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here. The Milne-faithful $\mathbb
1888	  G_a$-additive route (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, via $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$,
1889	  \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) reaches the same conclusion and is retained as an alternative,
1890	  but is \emph{not} on this critical path.
1891

1936	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
1937	
1938	\begin{proposition}
1939	
1940	\leanok
1941	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
1942	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1943	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1944	  \uses{def:genus}
1945	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
1946	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
1947	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
1948	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
1949	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
1950	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
1951	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
1952	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
1953	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
1954	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
1955	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
1956	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1957	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1958	\end{proposition}
1959	
1960	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
1961	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
1962	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
1963	\begin{proof}
1964	  \uses{def:genus}
1965	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
1966	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
1967	
1968	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
1969	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
1970	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
1971	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
1972	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
1973	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
1974	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
1975	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
1976	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
1977	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
1978	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1979	\end{proof}
1980	
1981	\begin{remark}
1982	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
1983	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
1984	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
1985	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
1986	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
1987	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
1988	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
1989	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
1990	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
1991	\end{remark}
1992	
1993	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
1994	
1995	\begin{theorem}
1996	
1997	\leanok
1998	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
1999	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
2000	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
2001	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
2002	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
2003	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
2004	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
2005	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
2006	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
2007	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
2008	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
2009	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
2010	
2011	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
2012	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
2013	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
2014	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
2015	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
2016	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
2017	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
2018	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
2019	  \eta[A]$.
2020	\end{theorem}
2021	
2022	\begin{proof}
2023	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
2024	  \leanok
2025	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
2026	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
2027	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
2028	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
2029	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
2030	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
2031	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
2032	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
2033	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
2034	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
2035	
2036	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
2037	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
2038	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
2039	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
2040	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
2041	
2042	  % NOTE (iter-166 review): proof body landed iter-166 via clean iso-transport
2043	  % (`obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus; set g := φ.inv ≫ f; obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ :=
2044	  % morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g; pin a₀ = η[A] via `toUnit_unique` basepoint transport;
2045	  % back-transport via `φ.hom_inv_id`'). `lean_verify` axioms $\{\mathtt{propext},
2046	  % \mathtt{sorryAx}, \mathtt{Classical.choice}, \mathtt{Quot.sound}\}$ — $\mathtt{sorryAx}$
2047	  % propagates honestly through (a) `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (RR-bridge sub-build, deferred to
2048	  % iter-167+) and (b) `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`'s 5 helper sorries. Lifts to axiom-clean
2049	  % once both upstreams discharge; checker confirmed no laundering.
2050	\end{proof}

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve $C$ over $\bar k$ of genus $g = g(C) > 0$ (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a $\bar k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}$ is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible $\bar k$-group scheme of dimension $g$; we write
73	$J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ and let $\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J$ denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen $\mathbf 1$-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	$J$.

78	The universal $T$-points of $J$ are degree-$0$ line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T$, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from $T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J$ of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A$ corresponding to a pointed morphism $\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	$(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$ defines a
87	regular morphism $\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$, the
88	birational morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J$ inverts to a
89	rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$, and the rational-map extension theorem

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, $k$ denotes a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ of relative dimension $1$. In other words, $C \to \Spec k$ is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$; see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$ is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the $k$-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of $C$.
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-$k$-modules construction and applying the $k$-linear sheaf cohomology $H^1$. The result is a $k$-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's $\ModuleCat k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier $\toModuleKSheaf C$ is the sheaf-of-$k$-modules constructed from $\struct C$ via the structure morphism; the wrapper $\HModule k F 1$ is the $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$, which carries a canonical $\Module k$-structure inherited from the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ returns the $k$-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme guarantees $\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty$, so $g(C)$ is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item $g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})$ (Serre duality).
45	  \item $g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)$, where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over $\mathbb C$, $g(C)$ equals the topological genus of $C(\mathbb C)$.
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition $\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ requires a $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of $\struct C$ on the topology of opens of the underlying space of $C$. Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\AddCommGrpCat$-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)$.
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel $\Module k$-valued construction with unit object $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$ instead of $\ULift\,\mathbb Z$, gluing through the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a $\Module k$ structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaf} (this project): for $C$ over $\Spec k$, the sheafification of the presheaf $U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))$ where the $k$-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{$H^0$ algebraic bridge} (this project): $H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)$, via Mathlib's $\Ext$ linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content $H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k$ on a connected proper $k$-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for $H^i(C, \mathcal F)$ on a proper $k$-curve.} Mathlib's $\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}$ provides $\Module.\mathtt{Finite}$ for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes $\mathtt{def\ genus}$, not $\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}$. Because $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$, $\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}$, and the structure-sheaf-as-$k$-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the $\mathtt{noncomputable}$ modifier on the $\mathtt{def}$. The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem $\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}$ in $\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}$ states that the Jacobian of $C$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$ --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ throughout. By a \emph{variety} over $\bar k$
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over $\bar k$
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over $\bar k$.
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to Y$ defined on a dense open subset
54	$U \subseteq X$, where two pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$, $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ are equivalent
55	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$ (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map $f$
56	is \emph{defined at} a point $x \in X$ if there is some representative $(U, \varphi_U)$
57	with $x \in U$; the set of such $x$ is an open subset $\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X$,
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of $f$.
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of $X$?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on $X$ or $Y$ the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if $Y$ is complete (proper over $\bar k$), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-$1$ point of a normal source $X$ (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if $Y$ is a group variety, the difference map
75	    $\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}$ on $X \times X$ has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For $Y$ an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-$1$ extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's $\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}$ API.
88

32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, a smooth variety $X$ over $\bar k$ (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over $\bar k$ that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety $A$ over $\bar k$ (in particular $A$ is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in $\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k$). A rational map
40	$f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to A$ defined on a dense open subset
42	$U \subseteq X$, two such pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$ and $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ being equivalent
43	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$.
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map $\alpha\colon V--\to A$ from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of $V$."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, let $A$ be
62	  an abelian variety over $\bar k$, and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ be a rational
63	  map. Then $f$ is defined on the whole of $X$: there is a unique regular morphism
64	  $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$ whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition $U \subseteq X$ of $f$ agrees with $\varphi_U$.
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from $X$ being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms $X \to A$ that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-$\geq 2$
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-$1$ structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let $U \subseteq X$ be a maximal open set of definition of $f$, and let
86	  $Z := X \setminus U$ be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim $\geq 2$), applied with the normal variety $X$ and the complete variety
90	      $A$, we have $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-$1$
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety $A$), the closed subset
93	      $Z$ is either empty or of pure codimension $1$ in $X$.
94	    \item Both conditions together force $Z = \emptyset$: a non-empty pure-codimension-$1$
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly $1$, contradicting $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore $U = X$ and $f$ admits a regular representative on all of $X$, which is the
98	  required regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$. Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-$1$ step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  $\mathcal O_{X, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ --- works because $X$ is normal
109	  and $A$ is complete) and a codim-$\geq 2$ step (depth-$\geq 2$ at codim-$\geq 2$ points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-$\geq 2$ input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring $(\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)$ of Krull dimension $d$, the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to $M = R$
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d$, so in particular
117	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2$ whenever $\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2$.
118	  Concretely, when $X$ is a smooth surface (e.g.\ $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ or
119	  $C \times C$ for a smooth projective curve $C$), every closed point $x$ is regular of
120	  dimension $2$, so $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is Cohen--Macaulay of depth $2$; sections of
121	  $\mathcal O_X$ on $U = X \setminus \{x\}$ extend uniquely across $x$, and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of $\mathcal O_A$ along $f$.
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-$\geq 2$ half. The dependency
126	  $\uses{thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}$ is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-$\geq 2$ extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-$\geq 2$ extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    $\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A$ and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on $A$ (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of $X$ (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-$1$ points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$. Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	$\varphi \colon C \to A$ through the canonical $f_P \colon C \to J$ as follows. The
161	symmetric power $C^{(g)}$ admits a regular morphism $\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A$
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J$ (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism $J \to A$ making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ followed by descent to $J$; the rational map
169	$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because $C^{(g)}$ is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and $A$ is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut ($J \cong J^\vee$) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
176	
177	\section{Lean encoding}
178	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
179	
180	The Lean target file is the new
181	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
182	this chapter targets is the theorem
183	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
184	notation):
185	\[
186	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over $\bar k$]} \;\to\;
187	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety $\bar k$} \;\to\;
188	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
189	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
190	\]
191	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
192	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
193	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
194	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
195	in the three-step pattern above (codim $\geq 2$ from A.4.a + pure-codim $1$ from A.4.a
196	$\Rightarrow$ empty indeterminacy locus).
197	
198	The Mathlib namespace target is
199	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
200	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
201	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
202	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
203	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
204	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
205	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
206	
207	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
208	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
209	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
210	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
211	
212	\section{Out of scope}
213	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
214	
215	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
216	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
217	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
218	\begin{itemize}
219	  \item \textbf{The codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself.}
220	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
221	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes it.
222	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
223	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
224	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
225	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
226	    (Milne~$\equiv$ Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
227	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
228	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this chapter only consumes them (indirectly,
229	    inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
230	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
231	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
232	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
233	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
234	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every $\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A$ is constant'',
235	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
236	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
237	    current routing: the genus-$0$ base case
238	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
239	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
240	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
241	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
242	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
243	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
244	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
245	    $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ to $C^{(g)} \to A$.
246	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
247	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
248	    project's positive-genus arm runs over $\bar k$ throughout (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}).
249	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
250	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
251	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$. It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X$ be a reduced geometrically irreducible $k$-scheme and let $Y$ be a separated $k$-scheme. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms of $k$-schemes, and let $U \subseteq X$ be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where $U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X$ is the canonical open immersion), then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms of $k$-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where $Y$ is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$ formed from the group law on $Y$. The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side $[\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ to $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$.
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{$Y \to \Spec k$ is separated.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ provides this directly (no projection from $\mathtt{IsProper}$ needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form $\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)$ that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{$X$ is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]$ over the field-spectrum base $\Spec k$ (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields $\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}$ via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion $U.\iota$ is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]$, the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality $g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}$ from the agreement-on-$U$ hypothesis. The promotion to $g_1 = g_2$ in $\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)$ uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of $X.\mathrm{left}$.
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion $U.\iota$ from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to $\mathrm{Over}$-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on $X$ and $Y$ are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-$\bar k$ argument (for $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ hitting the identity at a $\bar k$-point) to this theorem with $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $Y := A_{\bar k}$. The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property of $\Jac(C)$): given two morphisms $g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A$ with $g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P$, scheme-level agreement on the image of $\alpha_P$ (a non-empty open after passing to $C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)$) forces $g_1 = g_2$.
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of \ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the $\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)$-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

# Blueprint Review Report

## Slug
iter183

## Iteration
183

## Top-level summaries

### Lean difficulty quality

- `Picard_RelativeSpec.tex` / `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}`:
  the chapter prose pins a Yoneda-bijection statement
  $\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal A)) \cong
  \Hom_{\mathcal O_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal A, g_* \mathcal O_T)$, but the
  iter-173 file-skeleton landed it as the strictly weaker structural
  consequence `IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)` (acknowledged in
  iter-173 review NOTE inside the chapter). This is a documented
  intentional encoding gap, not a fabrication — the prose is correct, but
  Lane D consumers cannot rely on the natural-bijection conclusion until
  the Lean type is upgraded to `Functor.RepresentableBy`. Treat as **soon**
  (Lane D is iter-183-active but downstream of A.1.a, so the consequence
  is downstream slowdown, not a wrong proof).
- `Picard_RelativeSpec.tex` / `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}`:
  the Lean name `affine_base_iff` is misleading — the encoded type is the
  weaker `IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)` (acknowledged in the iter-173
  review NOTE in the chapter). Plan agent should track for the iter-174+
  signature refinement.
- `Picard_RelativeSpec.tex` / `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.base_change}`:
  the Lean type is the weaker existential `∃ 𝒜', Nonempty (pullback ≅ T.RelativeSpec 𝒜')`
  rather than the canonical iso with a named pullback `g^* 𝒜`
  (acknowledged in the iter-173 NOTE). Same soon-severity status.

All three findings are noted INSIDE the chapter prose by `% NOTE (iter-173 review)`
blocks — they are documented signature-drift items, not blueprint
correctness errors.

## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals

### Proposed chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`

**Covers**: `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean` (skeleton owed per
`STRATEGY.md` L143)
**Strategy phase**: A.3 — `Pic⁰` identity + degree (status "chapter pending; substrate unowned"
per `STRATEGY.md` L40)
**Why now**: A.3 has zero blueprint coverage at iter-183, blocking the
A.2.c → A.3 → A.4.d pipeline. Every other Route-A row has a chapter; A.3 is
the single missing node. Writing the chapter now lets the substrate
construction of `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (declared "NEW PROJECT
MATERIAL" by STRATEGY) be reviewed before any prover wastes work, and
unblocks the AlbaneseUP wiring downstream.

**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
1. `\definition` `\label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}` —
   The identity component (connected component of the identity section)
   of a group scheme $G$ over a base $S$, as an open and closed subgroup
   scheme. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}` [expected].
   Source: Stacks tag 0B7R / Milne *Abelian Varieties* §III.6 opening +
   SGA 3, Exposé VI_B §3.10. Probably needs `references/stacks-*.md` consult
   if Stacks coverage absent at pinned commit.
2. `\theorem` `\label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}` — For a group
   scheme $G$ locally of finite type over a field $k$, the identity
   component $G^0$ is an open subgroup scheme.
   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}` [expected].
   Source: Milne *Abelian Varieties* §III.6 (the standing decomposition);
   Stacks tag 0B7T (open and closed subgroup scheme construction).
3. `\definition` `\label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}` —
   $\Pic^0_{C/k} := (\Pic_{C/k})^0$, the identity component of the Picard
   scheme of `Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}` [expected].
   Source: Milne *Abelian Varieties* §III.6 Proposition 6.1 standing setup;
   Kleiman *Picard Scheme* §6.
4. `\definition` `\label{def:divisor_degree_pic}` — The degree map
   $\deg \colon \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb Z$ extracted from
   $\Pic_{C/k}$ via the Hilbert-polynomial stratification of the FGA proof.
   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}` [expected].
   Source: Kleiman *Picard Scheme* §5 (Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
   $\Pic_{X/S}$).
5. `\theorem` `\label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}` — $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is a
   smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme of dimension $g(C)$ —
   i.e.\ an abelian variety of dimension $g$.
   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}` [expected].
   Source: Milne *Abelian Varieties* §III.6 Theorem 6.4 (cf.\ Hartshorne IV.4).

**`\uses` skeleton**:
- `thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` uses `def:identity_component_group_scheme`
- `def:pic_zero_subscheme` uses `def:identity_component_group_scheme`,
  `def:pic_scheme` (from `Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`)
- `def:divisor_degree_pic` uses `def:pic_scheme`, `def:hilbert_polynomial`
  (from `Picard_QuotScheme.tex`)
- `thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety` uses `def:pic_zero_subscheme`,
  `thm:identity_component_open_subgroup`, `def:divisor_degree_pic`,
  `def:genus` (from `Genus.tex`), `thm:fga_pic_representability`

**Main theorem proof strategy**: The identity-component construction
extracts the open-and-closed subscheme containing $\eta_J$ from
representability of $\Pic_{C/k}$ as a disjoint union of quasi-projectives
(Theorem 4.1 of `Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex` already pins this
disjoint-union structure). Dimension is $g$ by Riemann–Roch / Serre duality
on $\Pic^0$ (Milne III.6); smoothness in characteristic $p$ requires
care (Milne IV §13–14) but in char $0$ is automatic. Geometric irreducibility
follows because $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the connected component of the identity by
construction. The degree map is the index of the Hilbert-polynomial
component containing a given class (Kleiman §5).

**References for writer**:
- `references/abelian-varieties.md` → `mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf`, §III.6
  + §III.13 — the standing decomposition of $\Pic_{C/k}$ into $\Pic^0$ and
  the degree map; smoothness in char-$p$ case.
- `references/kleiman-picard.md` → `kleiman-picard.pdf`, §5 (Hilbert-polynomial
  decomposition) and §6 (the identity component).
- `references/stacks-*.md` consult: Stacks tag 0B7R / 0B7T for the abstract
  group-scheme identity-component construction; the writer should retrieve
  these if not already in the local `stacks-*.md` cache.
- Optional: `references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.md` → IV.4 (the
  Jacobian of a curve as an abelian variety, char-0 case).

**Subphase choices exposed**:
- **Choice A vs Choice B**: build $\Pic^0$ as the open and closed identity-component
  subgroup scheme of the abstract group scheme (clean, uses Stacks-tag
  abstract API), vs build $\Pic^0$ directly as the moduli functor of
  degree-zero line bundles modulo pullback (concrete, matches Milne's
  exposition). Trade-off: Choice A inherits an abstract substrate gap
  (`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` is "NEW PROJECT MATERIAL" per STRATEGY,
  so needs its own ~100 LOC build); Choice B avoids that gap but couples
  $\Pic^0$ definition to the degree map, requiring degree to be set up
  before $\Pic^0$ even exists. **Recommendation**: Choice A — Stacks tag
  0B7R/0B7T is a self-contained standalone build, and the abstract
  identity-component API will be reusable for the future addition of
  $\Pic^0$'s dual $J^\vee$ (if ever needed for Albanese-UP variants).
- **Substrate split**: the underlying `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent`
  construction can be either part of this chapter or split off into a
  sibling `AlgebraicJacobian/GroupScheme/IdentityComponent.lean` (and a
  matching blueprint chapter). **Recommendation**: keep substrate in this
  chapter for iter-183 horizon, with a `% NOTE: future split candidate`
  marker — the substrate is small (~200–300 LOC per STRATEGY), and a
  separate chapter is premature until a second consumer materialises.
